AMAT.covers: FY25 → Q1 FY26 → Q2 FY26 (Dec 2025 – May 2026)
AMAT.headline: Applied Materials is capitalizing on three simultaneous transitions — HBM/DRAM mix shift, advanced packaging >50% growth, and gate-all-around at foundry — with Systems business guided >30% growth in FY26 and segment gross margins hitting 54.8%.

shifts.trend: FY25 Systems revenue +4% YoY → Q1 FY26 Systems -8% YoY (trailing-edge weakness) → FY26 full-year Systems guided >30% YoY (raised from mid-20s during Q2 call)
shifts.note: The Q1 dip was foundry/logic trailing-edge weakness; the FY26 >30% raise confirms that DRAM/HBM and advanced packaging are more than offsetting the soft non-leading logic demand.

shifts.trend: FY25 DRAM = 26% of Semiconductor Systems mix → Q1 FY26 DRAM = 34% (+8pts YoY); HBM wafer-area trade ratio moving from 3:1 to 4:1
shifts.note: The 4:1 HBM wafer-area conversion ratio versus standard DRAM is the structural tailwind that makes each memory node transition significantly more WFE-intensive for AMAT.

shifts.trend: Growing >50% in FY26, representing multiple billions; AMAT holds #1 vendor position; hybrid bonding platform expanding TAM via X-ray inspection acquisition
shifts.note: Advanced packaging becoming a multi-billion sub-segment with data-center wafer starts set to pass smartphone wafer starts in 2027 — structurally expands AMAT's SAM per unit of WFE.

shifts.trend: AGS +3% in FY25 → +15% YoY in Q1 FY26 → FY26 guided mid-to-high-teens growth (raised from low double-digits at Q2 FY26 call); >35,000 chambers connected to AI-enabled servers
shifts.note: Industry utilization at cycle highs is driving spares and service-agreement pull-through; the subscription-model long-term agreements create durable revenue below the systems cycle.

shifts.trend: China 37% of FY24 revenue → 30% FY25 → ~24% Q2 FY26; advanced restricted tech <10% of total; ICAP market (IoT/comm/auto/power) expected flat to slightly up FY26
shifts.note: China concentration risk is declining structurally as AMAT's leading-edge mix shifts toward Taiwan, Korea, and the US — partially offsetting the BIS export-control compliance settlement ($253M in Q1 FY26) and removing the largest tail risk.

shifts.trend: FY25 consolidated GM 48.7% → Q1 FY26 consolidated GM 49.0% → Q2 FY26 systems segment GM 54.8% (first-time disclosure); company-wide trending above 50%
shifts.note: The segment-margin disclosure at 54.8% confirms that product mix improvement toward leading-edge and advanced packaging is a structural margin driver, not a one-time event.

AMAT.verdict: Thesis intact with visibility improving: AMAT has the broadest exposure to the three key inflection points in the 2026 WFE cycle (DRAM/HBM, advanced packaging, GAA foundry) and is seeing broad demand confirmation across all three simultaneously for the first time. The primary near-term risk is that China (~24% of revenue) faces further export-control tightening, while the BIS settlement ($253M in Q1) signals continued compliance friction; however, the shrinking China concentration means each incremental restriction matters less to the bull case.

AMD.covers: FY25 (10-K) → Q4 FY25 → Q1 FY26 → Q2 FY26 guidance (Feb 4 – May 6, 2026)
AMD.headline: AMD's data center flywheel is accelerating — MI350 GPU + 5th-gen EPYC pairing landed a 6 GW OpenAI + Meta book of business while margins expand to 55%+ as China headwinds fade.

shifts.trend: FY25 DC $16.6B (+32% YoY) → Q1 FY26 DC $5.8B (+57% YoY, +7% QoQ) → Q2 FY26 guided ~$6.4B+ implied on total guide of $11.2B (+46% YoY)
shifts.note: YoY growth rate has nearly doubled from 32% (FY25) to 57% (Q1 FY26) as MI350/450 ramp displaces Hopper-era deployments and EPYC gains share.

shifts.trend: 4th consecutive EPYC record in Q1 FY26 → >50% YoY EPYC growth → CPU-to-GPU ratio moving from 1:8 toward parity; server CPU TAM raised to $120B by 2030
shifts.note: Agentic AI inference is reinserting CPU as orchestration layer, expanding AMD's addressable market at the same time GPU revenue scales.

shifts.trend: FY25 GM 50% → Q1 FY26 non-GAAP GM 55% (+3pts YoY) → Q2 FY26 guided ~56%
shifts.note: DC revenue mix now ~56% of total; higher-value Instinct GPUs and premium EPYC SKUs are lifting blended margins despite aggressive opex investment.

shifts.trend: $800M MI308 inventory charge in Q2 FY25 → $360M partial reversal in Q4 FY25 → Feb 2026 MI325 China licenses granted (with 25% re-import tariff)
shifts.note: Partial license relief reduces overhang but the 25% tariff and ongoing inspection regime keep ex-China demand as the cleaner growth driver.

shifts.trend: OpenAI 6 GW multi-year purchase agreement (Oct 2025) → Meta partnership up to 6 GW MI Instinct announced Q1 FY26 → MI450/Helios rack sampling broadly underway
shifts.note: Dual hyperscale anchor agreements give AMD GPU visibility into late FY26 and FY27, reducing concentration risk and providing a credible Blackwell alternative narrative.

shifts.trend: FY25 FCF not separately flagged → Q1 FY26 FCF $2.6B (record); cash $12.3B
shifts.note: Record quarterly FCF demonstrates operating leverage is materializing even as R&D (+39% YoY) and MG&A (+41% YoY) ramp for the AI investment cycle.

AMD.verdict: AMD's DC thesis is intact and gaining credibility: dual 6 GW anchor agreements with OpenAI and Meta, a 57% YoY Q1 growth print, and an expanding margin profile all confirm MI-series competitive traction. Key risk is execution on the MI450/Helios ramp — Blackwell is fully deployed and NVDA will launch Vera Rubin in Q3 FY27, so any delay risks losing the window; China export-control uncertainty and $2.4B quarterly R&D burn are secondary but real drags on free cash conversion.

AMKR.headline: Amkor's AI-driven advanced packaging cycle is inflecting sharply — record Q1 revenue (+27% YoY), AI DC revenue tripling, and a 3x capex surge signal a multi-year capacity buildout is underway.

shifts.trend: FY25 +6% YoY → Q1 FY26 +27% YoY; Q2 FY26 guided $1.75B-$1.85B (implied +30%+ YoY)
shifts.note: Broad-based acceleration across all four end markets with AI-related computing and Communications leading at +19% and +42% respectively.

shifts.trend: Low-50s% in Q1 2025 → low-70s% by Q1 FY26 — approximately 20 percentage point improvement in two quarters
shifts.note: Utilization recovery is the key margin driver; headroom to high-70s/80s as AI packaging ramps means further gross margin upside ahead.

shifts.trend: FY25 full-year 14.0% (vs 14.8% FY24) → Q1 FY26 14.2% (+2.3pts YoY) → Q2 FY26 guided 14.5%-15.5%
shifts.note: Vietnam Facility ramp headwind that compressed FY25 margins is dissipating; higher utilization and AI packaging mix driving sequential expansion.

shifts.trend: Still ramping Q4 FY25 → HDFO CPU ramp commenced Q1 FY26 → management committed to tripling AI DC revenue in full-year 2026
shifts.note: HDFO heterogeneous CPU packaging with the lead customer (Apple) is the primary AI vector; HBM/2.5D integration and SiPh/CPO capability broadens the pipeline beyond one customer.

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $904.6M (13.5% of sales) → FY26 guided $2.5B-$3.0B (37-45% of FY25 sales); Q1 FY26 capex $225M vs $80M Q1 2025 (+181% YoY)
shifts.note: Largest capex plan in company history; Arizona Phase 1 construction underway with manufacturing targeted H1 2028 — CHIPS Act $407M direct funding and 35% ITC reduce cash burden.

shifts.trend: Apple 29.8% and Qualcomm 11.1% of FY25 sales; top-10 = 72% — concentration unchanged QoQ
shifts.note: Single-customer concentration remains the principal downside risk; Arizona fab serves US onshoring demand and may diversify the base over 2027-2028 as capacity comes online.

AMKR.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating: AMKR is transitioning from a consumer-driven OSAT to an AI advanced packaging platform company, with utilization, margins, and AI DC revenue all inflecting simultaneously. The $2.5B-$3B FY26 capex plan — funded partly by CHIPS Act incentives — cements a multi-year capacity advantage in heterogeneous integration, but execution risk on Arizona and customer concentration in Apple remain the key risks to monitor.

AMZN.headline: AWS re-accelerated to 28% growth on a $150B run rate while Q1 capex hit $43.2B and the OpenAI $100B expansion pushed the RPO to $364B — demand visibly outrunning supply.

shifts.trend: $77.7B FY24 → $128.3B FY25 → $43.2B in Q1 FY26 alone (~$170B+ annualized pace)
shifts.note: The Q1 FY26 capex already represents 78% of the prior full-year Q1 base ($24.3B Q1 2025), and management guided FY26 to increase further, implying the $100B full-year mark is a floor, not a ceiling.

shifts.trend: AWS $108.5B FY24 → $128.7B FY25 (+20%) → $37.6B Q1 FY26 (+28% YoY, fastest in 15 quarters)
shifts.note: The Q1 FY26 acceleration to 28% — against the largest sequential base in AWS history — signals genuine demand expansion rather than easy compares.

shifts.trend: $244B at Dec 31 2025 → $364B at Mar 31 2026 (+49% in a single quarter)
shifts.note: The $120B RPO jump in Q1 was driven by OpenAI's $100B expansion of its existing $38B AWS commitment, adding Trainium chip obligations; the figure still excludes a subsequent $50B OpenAI commitment and $5B Anthropic compute facility.

shifts.trend: Trainium + Graviton annualized run rate >$20B with ~40% QoQ growth in Q1 FY26; Bedrock token volume in Q1 exceeded all prior years combined
shifts.note: Custom silicon revenue growing triple digits YoY is a structural supply-chain signal: demand for Trainium clusters is pulling through power, networking, and substrate capacity well ahead of GPU alternatives.

shifts.trend: Trainium2 ~30% better price-performance than comparable GPUs; Trainium3 shipped Q1 2026 and was nearly fully subscribed at launch
shifts.note: Management's claim that Trainium will save 'tens of billions of dollars of CapEx per year' at scale versus third-party chips, if validated, compresses the total GPU TAM while sustaining or expanding total data-center infrastructure spend.

shifts.trend: AWS operating margin 39.4% Q1 2025 → 37.7% Q1 2026 despite $43.2B capex quarter; FY25 AWS operating income $45.6B (35.4%)
shifts.note: The slight margin dip reflects the upfront depreciation weight of the infra build; the underlying margin structure remains intact, underpinning the investment case for continued capex escalation.

AMZN.verdict: Amazon's AI infrastructure cycle is in full acceleration: capex nearly doubled YoY in FY25 and the Q1 FY26 run rate implies a $170B+ annualized pace, while AWS re-accelerated to 28% growth on a $150B base and RPO jumped $120B in a single quarter. For the inference stack supply chain, AWS's aggressive dual track — third-party GPU procurement (NVIDIA) plus proprietary Trainium at >$20B annualized run rate — means demand is deep and broad across GPU, custom silicon, power, and data-center capacity layers. The $364B RPO (and growing) provides multi-year revenue visibility that locks in capex commitments well into 2028, making AWS one of the most durable pull-through demand signals in the entire stack.

ANET.headline: Arista accelerated from $9.0B FY25 to a $11.5B FY26 guide while demand structurally outstripped supply — deferred revenue, purchase commitments, and lead times all blew out to record levels.

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $9.0B (+28.6% YoY) → Q1 FY26 run-rate $10.8B ($2.71B/qtr, +35.1% YoY) → FY26 guide raised to ~$11.5B (~28% growth)
shifts.note: YoY revenue acceleration from 28.6% to 35.1% in one quarter confirms AI Ethernet adoption is pulling forward spend, not just sustaining prior pace.

shifts.trend: Purchase commitments $6.8B (Q4 FY25) → $8.9B (Q1 FY26, +31%); chip lead times extended to 52 weeks with multi-year reservation needs
shifts.note: Industry-wide silicon, optics, and memory shortages expected to persist 1-2 years; Arista is absorbing gross-margin pressure to pre-commit supply rather than lose share.

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 64.1% → Q1 FY26 product gross margin 61.9% (reported 62.4% blended); FY26 guide 62%-64%
shifts.note: Large-customer pricing discounts plus supply-chain procurement premiums are structurally compressing margins 150-200bps from FY25 peak — manageable but directional.

shifts.trend: FY26 revenue guide: original ~$10.5B → raised to ~$11.5B; AI fabric target: $3.25B → $3.5B (>2x FY25 AI sales implied)
shifts.note: Double guidance raises within one earnings cycle signal that AI Ethernet trials are converting to committed deployments faster than management modeled.

shifts.trend: FY25: two end customers 26% + 16% of revenue; 2026 outlook calls for 1-2 new hyperscaler 10%+ customers joining Microsoft and Meta
shifts.note: Broadening from 2 to potentially 4 hyperscaler anchors reduces single-customer event risk while the >100 cumulative 800 GbE customer count expands the enterprise tail.

shifts.trend: Deferred revenue $2.79B (FY24) → $5.37B (FY25) → $6.20B (Q1 FY26, +$826M in one quarter); RPO $7.7B at Q1 FY26 end
shifts.note: Ballooning deferred revenue from AI Ethernet acceptance-period contracts acts as a binding demand signal — shipments will be recognized over 2026-2027 regardless of order softness.

ANET.verdict: The AI inference-networking thesis for ANET is intact and strengthening: two guidance raises, accelerating revenue growth, and $8.9B in purchase commitments show hyperscaler AI fabric spend is durable. The primary risk is gross-margin dilution from supply procurement premiums and large-customer discounts, which could keep margins 150-200bps below FY25 peak for 1-2 years. The key catalyst to watch is scale-up Ethernet (ESUN) converting from trial to production in 2027-2028, which could open a new revenue layer beyond current scale-out wins.

AVGO.covers: FY25 (10-K) → Q1 FY26 → Q2 FY26 → Q3 FY26 guidance (Dec 18, 2025 – Jun 9, 2026)
AVGO.headline: AVGO's AI semiconductor revenue is doubling every two quarters — from $8.4B in Q1 FY26 to a guided $16B in Q3, with six custom XPU customers and $30B in-quarter bookings creating unprecedented near-term visibility.

shifts.trend: FY25 AI semi ~$12-15B implied → Q1 FY26 $8.4B (+106% YoY) → Q2 FY26 $10.8B (+143% YoY) → Q3 FY26 guided $16B (+200%+ YoY); FY26 full-year guide $56B
shifts.note: The step-up from $10.8B to $16B in a single quarter is unprecedented in AVGO history and reflects multi-GW XPU delivery windows for Google, Anthropic, Meta, and now OpenAI simultaneously.

shifts.trend: FY25: 5 customers → Q1 FY26: 6th customer added (OpenAI, >1 GW in 2027) → all 6 programmes scaling to multiple GW
shifts.note: OpenAI's entry as a sixth hyperscale XPU customer structurally de-risks revenue concentration and adds a $10B+ annualized incremental revenue stream into FY27.

shifts.area: AI networking share within AI revenue
shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 networking ~33% of AI revenue (+60% YoY) → Q2 FY26 networking ~40% of AI revenue; Tomahawk 7 (200T) taping out in Q2
shifts.note: Networking attach within the AI stack is rising, reinforcing moat depth — AVGO captures both the compute silicon and the scale-out interconnect dollar.

shifts.trend: Prior FY27 guide 'in excess of $100B' (chips only) reiterated → extended to FY28 with continued growth; $30B in-quarter Q2 bookings vs $10.8B shipped signals 3x backlog
shifts.note: Booking-to-billing ratio of ~2.8x in Q2 provides nine-plus months of revenue visibility — effectively de-risking FY26 H2 and pre-selling FY27 capacity.

shifts.trend: FY25 GM 68% → Q1 FY26 GM 68% (flat) → Q2 FY26 GM 77.1% (software mix pop) → Q3 FY26 guided ~74% (semi mix dilution returning)
shifts.note: As AI semi ramp grows faster than software, blended GM will compress toward the mid-60s through FY27 — management has explicitly flagged XPU rack leasing as a future gross-margin dilutant.

shifts.trend: FY25 software $27.0B (+26%) → Q1 FY26 $6.8B (+1% YoY, decelerating) → Q3 FY26 guided $8.9B (+31% YoY, re-accelerating)
shifts.note: VMware VCF 9.1 with heterogeneous GPU support is pulling software ARR growth back above 20%+, adding a high-margin counter-weight to the lower-GM semi ramp.

AVGO.verdict: AVGO is in the strongest forward-visibility position of any company in the AI semiconductor stack — $56B FY26 AI guide, $30B Q2 backlog, and six funded GW-scale customers gives a multi-year order book that rivals have not matched. The primary risk is gross-margin compression as XPU racks transition to a lower-margin pass-through delivery model, but operating margin is stable at ~67%, suggesting earnings quality is intact; concentration in top-5 customers at 50% of revenue is the secondary structural risk.

CAT.headline: Caterpillar's order backlog jumped 71% to $51.2B with Power & Energy leading, and Power Generation sales are accelerating (+32% FY25, +22% Q1 FY26) as data centers increasingly pursue prime-power solutions — but tariff costs are the new margin friction.

shifts.trend: Backlog $30.0B at YE24 → $51.2B at YE25 (+71%), with largest increase in Power & Energy; $19.3B not expected to fill in 2026
shifts.note: A 71% backlog surge in a single fiscal year is exceptional at Caterpillar's scale; the $19.3B portion stretching beyond 2026 provides two-plus years of revenue visibility and signals that data center prime-power orders are not a 2025-vintage blip.

shifts.trend: Power Generation FY25 $10.3B (+32% YoY) → Power & Energy Q1 FY26 $7.0B (+22% YoY) with continued data center prime power demand cited
shifts.note: Growth is broad-based across large reciprocating engines for data centers and oil & gas, with prime power (as backup and increasingly primary) emerging as a distinct demand category management calls out explicitly.

shifts.trend: P&E profit margin FY25 19.9% (flat despite $972M volume tailwind offset by $919M manufacturing cost headwinds) → Q1 FY26 overall operating margin 17.7% vs 18.1%; tariff costs $700M Q2 2026 alone
shifts.note: Margin expansion is being suppressed by tariff costs ($2.2–2.4B FY26 guidance) and manufacturing inefficiencies from rapid capacity ramp — volume leverage is real but being absorbed by input costs, not flowing through.

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: first explicit 'prime power orders trending higher'; Q1 FY26 10-Q: 'continue to see demand for prime power trend higher as data center customers look for alternative power solutions'
shifts.note: Two consecutive filings with consistent language about an emerging prime-power category (not just backup generation) signals a structural demand shift; data centers building behind-the-meter gas generation is a new end-market for CAT.

shifts.trend: $919M manufacturing cost headwind in FY25 P&E → $1.0B IEEPA tariff costs incurred in Q1 FY26 (refund recovery not deemed probable despite SCOTUS ruling); Q2 2026 tariff costs guided ~$700M
shifts.note: The tariff overhang is meaningful and not going away: even after the SCOTUS IEEPA ruling, CAT is not booking a receivable, and tariff costs remain a $2.2–2.4B FY26 drag that makes true operating leverage hard to see in reported margins.

CAT.verdict: Caterpillar's exposure to data-center power generation is real and growing — the $51.2B backlog and explicit prime-power demand commentary across two filings confirm that CAT is a direct beneficiary of the AI infrastructure buildout, not merely a cyclical industrial name. The thesis is intact with strong near-term revenue visibility. Key risks are tariff-driven margin compression (a $2.2–2.4B headwind is significant even for a company of CAT's scale) and the IEEPA refund uncertainty; if tariffs persist and the data center prime-power cycle turns, the combined margin pressure could be severe. The $19.3B backlog extending beyond 2026 is the best evidence that demand durability is underpriced.

CDNS.headline: Cadence accelerated to 19% revenue growth in Q1 FY26 with China surging +36% YoY, backlog expanding to $8.0B, and AI-driven custom silicon complexity powering verification tool demand at a pace management says is unprecedented.

shifts.note: Acceleration driven by AI chip design complexity at N3/N2 nodes requiring 3–5x more verification compute than prior-generation designs.

shifts.trend: FY25 RPO $7.8B (53% in next 12mo) → Q1 FY26 $8.0B (55% in next 12mo)
shifts.note: Backlog expansion and rising near-term mix (55% vs 53%) signals customers locking in multi-year EDA commitments as AI design starts accelerate.

shifts.trend: FY25 China $680M +19% YoY (13% of revenue); Q1 FY26 China $189M +36% YoY (13% mix); BIS license whiplash (May 23 imposed, Jul 2 rescinded) now behind
shifts.note: Company flags 'limited' impact from current trade controls but the Sep 2025 50%-owned-affiliate rule creates a latent risk that could clip future China design-starts.

shifts.trend: FY25 operating margin 28% (vs 29% FY24, pressured by $128.5M BIS/DOJ settlement) → Q1 FY26 operating margin 29%
shifts.note: Margin normalized after the one-time settlement charge; geographic growth diversification (EMEA +23%, Japan +36%) reduces dependence on any single region.

shifts.trend: Cadence Palladium/Protium demand described as growing 'faster than any prior technology cycle'; FY26 guidance raised to ~$5.1B–$5.15B (+8–9% implied) post-Q1 beat
shifts.note: Hyperscaler ASIC tape-outs at N3/N2 consume multiples of prior-gen verification compute, making Cadence hardware-emulation a secular-growth category independent of unit chip volumes.

shifts.trend: Hexagon D&E (MSC Nastran) acquisition announced in FY25; revolving credit drawn for close; Arm Artisan IP, VLAB Works, Secure-IC, agentic verification tech also added in FY25
shifts.note: Cadence is executing a simultaneous M&A + integration cycle across simulation, IP, and security — risk of resource distraction at exactly the moment EDA demand is peaking.

CDNS.verdict: Thesis intact and arguably strengthening: custom silicon proliferation (hyperscaler ASICs, edge AI) is compressing design cycles and raising verification intensity, which flows directly to Cadence's Palladium/Protium franchises. The $8.0B backlog growing with rising near-term concentration (55%) gives multi-quarter earnings visibility that is unusual for an EDA company. Key risk is China — while Q1 FY26 showed +36% YoY growth (BIS whiplash resolved), the Sep 2025 50%-owned-affiliate rule and potential future controls remain a structural overhang on the ~13% China revenue mix. The simultaneous Hexagon D&E/Arm IP/Secure-IC integration workload is a second-order execution risk that the market is not pricing at current multiples.

CEG.headline: Calpine close transformed CEG into a 55 GW power giant with >20% EPS CAGR through 2029, anchored by nuclear PTC and a hyperscaler offtake pipeline that keeps expanding.

shifts.trend: Nuclear-only fleet (Q3 FY25) → 55 GW combined platform post-Calpine close Jan 7, 2026; capacity roughly doubled
shifts.note: Calpine acquisition completed, adding 21 GW natural gas, 730 MW geothermal, and 800 MW battery alongside the existing 21 GW nuclear fleet, making CEG the largest US power generator.

shifts.trend: Adj EPS $9.39 FY25 → FY26 guide $11–12/share (initiated Feb 2026, affirmed May 2026); Calpine contributes ~$2/share; FCF before growth $8.4B FY26–27 → $11.5–13B FY28–29
shifts.note: Earnings trajectory is stepping up materially with Calpine accretion, and management's outer-year FCF guide implies a ~45% step-up from the FY26–27 base — the nuclear PTC floor plus capacity-price tailwinds underpin visibility.

shifts.trend: One Meta PPA (Clinton, Jun 2025) → Crane/TMI restart (Microsoft 20-yr PPA, $1B DOE loan Nov 2025) → CyrusOne powered-land deal (PUCT approved) → 5,000 MW new capacity submitted to PJM queue for data center offtake (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: Each filing added a new hyperscaler anchor or regulatory milestone; management cited hyperscaler 2026 capex ~75% higher than FY25 and still being revised upward.

shifts.trend: FERC/PUCT/NYPSC Calpine approvals (by Jul 2025) → DOE $1B loan for Crane (Nov 2025) → NY PSC 20-yr ZEC extension (Jan 2026) → PJM backstop framework on track for FERC submission Jun 2026
shifts.note: The regulatory runway has progressively de-risked both the acquisition integration and the nuclear fleet extension through 2049; colocation clarity on PJM is the remaining catalyst.

shifts.trend: Clinton 30 MW uprate (by 2029) → Crane 835 MW restart (targeting 2027 capacity credit) → Byron and Braidwood uprates planned (EPS accretion 2030+) → 5,000 MW PJM queue submission
shifts.note: The capacity pipeline is widening across filings, shifting from single-site relicensing to a multi-site uprate-and-restart program; nuclear uprates qualify for the IRA 45Y clean electricity PTC.

shifts.trend: No buyback data in Q3 FY25 → $335M repurchased at ~$285/share post-Q1 FY26 on $5B authorization; dividend growing 10%/yr
shifts.note: FCF generation from the combined platform is large enough to fund both the growth pipeline and significant shareholder returns simultaneously.

CEG.verdict: The thesis is intact and strengthening: CEG has successfully assembled the largest clean-power platform in the US precisely as AI-driven baseload demand is inflecting, with long-term hyperscaler PPAs providing a durable earnings floor on top of the nuclear PTC. The primary risk is execution on Calpine integration and PJM colocation rule-making; a colocation framework adverse to behind-the-meter nuclear deals would delay the outer-year FCF ramp. The $11.5–13B FY28–29 FCF guide — affirmed across two consecutive filings — is the key datapoint to monitor.

CLS.headline: Celestica's hyperscaler networking and compute programs re-accelerated to +53% revenue growth in Q1 FY26 with record 8.0% operating margin, and management raised full-year guidance to $17–19B while disclosing 10 active 1.6T switch programs and the first CPO switch win.

shifts.trend: CCS +42% FY25; HPS (networking switches + AI compute) +81% to $5.0B, 41% of total → Q1 FY26 CCS +76%, HPS +63% to ~$1.7B, 42% of total; total company +53% YoY
shifts.note: The acceleration is driven by two simultaneous ramps: 800G switch volumes (+69% communications) and the recovery of the enterprise AI/ML compute program that paused in 2025 (+101% enterprise in Q1 FY26).

shifts.trend: Q4 FY25 CCS margin 8.4% → Q1 FY26 CCS margin 8.6% (vs 8.0% Q1 FY25); adj operating margin Q1 FY26 8.0% record; FY26 guide raised to ~8.1%
shifts.note: Margins are expanding even as mix shifts toward higher-revenue-per-unit HPS programs; operating leverage from scale and manufacturing maturity on established programs is now visible in the reported numbers.

shifts.trend: HPS 29% of FY24 revenue → 41% of FY25 revenue → 42% of Q1 FY26 revenue; HPS growing faster than CCS overall in every period
shifts.note: HPS (hardware platform solutions — high-value integrated rack-scale compute and networking) becoming the majority of the CCS segment is a structural margin tailwind; it commands higher ASPs and stickier customer relationships.

shifts.trend: FY25: 800G switch ramp underway → Q1 FY26: 10 active 1.6T programs in development; CPO switch win on Broadcom Tomahawk 6 (production H2 2027); AMD Helios digital-native rack collaboration (Q1 2027); AMD CPO win not previously disclosed in FY25 10-K
shifts.note: Celestica is deliberately positioning at the leading edge of two successive switching transitions (800G now, 1.6T in 2026–2027, CPO in 2027+) — program wins at design-in stage create 2–3 year revenue visibility even before production starts.

shifts.trend: No explicit FY26 revenue guide in FY25 10-K → FY26 raised at Q1 earnings to $17–19B (implied +40–55% YoY from FY25's $12.2B); adj EPS raised to $8.75–10.15; Q2 guide $4.15–4.45B
shifts.note: The magnitude of the guidance raise ($17–19B implies adding $5–7B of revenue in a single year) signals that hyperscaler program commitments are locked in, not speculative.

shifts.trend: FY25: 3 customers each >10% (32%, 14%, 12%); top 10 = 79% → Q1 FY26: 3 customers each >10% (35%, 15%, 15%); top customer share rising
shifts.note: Revenue is growing but concentration is intensifying, not diversifying; the largest customer at 35% of Q1 FY26 revenue is a single-point-of-failure risk if any hyperscaler pauses capex or insources manufacturing.

CLS.verdict: The Celestica thesis is executing at the top end of expectations: revenue re-acceleration (+53% Q1 FY26), record margins, and a raised FY26 guide to $17–19B all confirm that the 800G switch ramp and AI compute recovery are both running simultaneously. The pipeline to 1.6T programs and the CPO switch win extend the growth runway well into 2027–2028. The primary risk is hyperscaler concentration — three customers account for 65% of revenue, and any program pause (as happened with the enterprise AI/ML customer in 2025) creates visible revenue holes. Supply chain constraints across custom silicon, memory, 40+ layer PCBs, and optical components are the near-term execution risk as the company scales at unprecedented speed.

CMI.headline: Cummins' Power Systems segment is running at record margins driven by data-center demand that now stretches 6–8 quarters out, while the legacy truck engine business drags the headline but masks the infrastructure upgrade story.

shifts.trend: Power Systems FY25 $7.46B (+16%), Power Generation sub-segment $4.73B (+19%) → Q1 FY26 Power Systems $1.96B (+19% YoY), Power Generation $1.28B (+28%)
shifts.note: Power Generation growth is re-accelerating into Q1 FY26 even off a higher base, with North America and China both cited as demand drivers — the data center demand runway is not yet showing deceleration.

shifts.trend: FY25 Power Systems EBITDA margin 22.7% (from 18.4% in FY24, +430 bps) → Q1 FY26 Power Systems EBITDA margin 29.5% (+590 bps vs Q1 FY25's 23.6%)
shifts.note: The margin expansion is substantial and accelerating — a 590 bps jump in a single quarter reflects operating leverage on the data-center-driven volume surge, not just mix; this is now well above the segment's historical range.

shifts.trend: Power Systems EBITDA FY25 $1.69B (+44% YoY) → Q1 FY26 Power Systems EBITDA $577M (+48% YoY)
shifts.note: Absolute dollar EBITDA growth in Power Systems is accelerating on a percentage basis even as the base grows — data center demand extending 6–8 quarters gives management confidence in sustaining the capacity investment cycle.

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: data center backlog 6–8 quarters out → Q1 FY26 10-Q: maintains 'continued strength in power generation' language; no extension or contraction of backlog commentary
shifts.note: Management has not shortened the 6–8 quarter demand horizon across two consecutive filings, suggesting the order book remains full well into 2027–2028 despite macro uncertainty.

shifts.trend: FY25 $458M Accelera charges (electrolyzer impairment + exit); FY25 net income $2.8B vs $3.9B prior year; Q1 FY26 Engine segment EBITDA -$179M YoY on lower truck volumes and product coverage costs
shifts.note: The exit from electrolyzers is a strategic clarification — Cummins is doubling down on reciprocating power generation rather than hydrogen, which aligns the capital allocation squarely behind the data center tailwind.

CMI.verdict: The data center power generation thesis is executing cleanly at both the revenue and margin line, with Power Systems margins hitting 29.5% in Q1 FY26 — a level that, if sustained, would re-rate the segment's earnings contribution relative to consensus expectations built around historical 18–22% margins. The main risk is concentration: Power Systems is masking weakness in Engine (trucks) and the Accelera write-down, and any data center demand pause would expose the underlying industrial cyclicality. Tariff costs ($2.2–2.4B FY26 guidance) are the margin wildcard and could compress the Power Systems beat if they land in the supply chain.

COHR.headline: Coherent's D&C segment accelerated to +41% YoY with NVIDIA's $2B equity investment slashing leverage from 1.7x to 0.5x — the company has pivoted from a leveraged conglomerate to a pure-play AI optics supplier one quarter ahead of its own InP capacity schedule.

shifts.trend: D&C revenue: $1.21B (+34% YoY, Q2 FY26) → $1.36B (+41% YoY, Q3 FY26); Datacenter sub-segment +37% YoY and +13% QoQ in Q3; Communications (DCI/telecom) +60% YoY Q3
shifts.note: D&C acceleration from 34% to 41% YoY while a new 'scale across' category entered the mix in Q3 signals demand is broadening beyond point-to-point DCI into full AI fabric interconnect.

shifts.trend: Non-GAAP gross margin: ~39% (Q2 FY26) → 39.6% (Q3 FY26, +57bps QoQ); D&C segment profit +49% YoY Q3; Q4 FY26 guide 39%-41% non-GAAP GM
shifts.note: InP capacity expansion completing one quarter early is enabling volume leverage that translates directly to margin improvement — Q4 guide midpoint of 40% would be a meaningful step above recent peaks.

shifts.trend: InP laser capacity doubling: tracking one quarter ahead of schedule (Q2 FY26 commentary) → confirmed completed in Q3 FY26 ahead of original timeline
shifts.note: Early completion of the InP capacity doubling is the key execution milestone differentiating Coherent from LITE: COHR is adding supply, whereas LITE is still constrained, shifting relative competitive position for 2H 2026.

shifts.trend: Net leverage ratio: 1.7x (Q2 FY26) → 0.5x (Q3 FY26) after NVIDIA $2B equity investment; CapEx $290M in Q3 (elevated but manageable on the new cash base); cash $3B
shifts.note: Leverage collapse from 1.7x to 0.5x in a single quarter removes the balance-sheet overhang that had been the primary investor concern — COHR can now self-fund capacity expansion without dilutive equity.

shifts.trend: Portfolio narrowing toward AI optics through divestitures: aerospace divested Sep 2025, Munich Germany business divested Jan 2026; D&C now 75% of revenue vs. prior mixed industrial/defense exposure
shifts.note: Two divestitures in six months deliberately sharpening focus toward the AI optics thesis — management is explicitly betting the company on the D&C/AI infrastructure wave.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 actual $1.81B vs Q3 guide ~$1.8B (in-line); Q4 FY26 guide $1.91B-$2.05B representing +6% to +13% sequential growth; OCS TAM cited >$4B, CPO TAM >$15B
shifts.note: Q4 guidance mid-point of ~$1.98B would mark the first sub-quarter of $2B revenue for Coherent — and the CPO/OCS TAM framing sets the stage for a multi-year re-rating if those markets open in 2027-2028.

COHR.verdict: Coherent's AI inference thesis is sharply strengthening: InP capacity expanding ahead of schedule, leverage collapsed post-NVIDIA investment, and the D&C segment accelerating to +41% YoY while divestitures focus the portfolio directly on AI datacenter optics. The company is the most vertically integrated optical supplier for AI (InP + SiPh + packaging), giving it a credible moat for OCS and CPO markets if those adopt on the 2027-2028 timeline management cites. The key risk is execution: COHR must sustain margin expansion through Q4 and into FY27 to prove the GM trajectory is structural rather than a one-quarter mix benefit — any miss on the $1.91B-$2.05B Q4 guide would reset the narrative.

CRM.headline: Agentforce crossed 1.6B agentic work units in Q1 FY27 with Slackbot AWUs +300% QoQ, but Headless 360 commercial model remains undefined — Salesforce is proving consumption at scale faster than it is monetizing it.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26: Agentforce consumption pricing flagged as 'relatively new and uncertain' → Q4 FY26: AWU growth >300% QoQ in Slackbot → Q1 FY27: 1.6B+ total AWUs; sales agents +200% QoQ
shifts.note: AWU growth confirms genuine enterprise adoption at scale, but consumption-to-revenue conversion lag is the critical unknown for modeling.

shifts.trend: Agentforce One + Agentforce for Apps bookings +60% YoY in Q1 FY27; premium tier ARPU 60–80% higher vs base CRM editions
shifts.note: Premium tier attach is the clearest near-term monetization signal — customers upgrading to Agentforce editions generate immediate ARPU uplift without waiting for consumption metering.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 RPO $59.5B (down from $63.4B at Jan 2025) → FY26 10-K total RPO $72.4B (+14% YoY); cRPO $35.1B (+16% YoY)
shifts.note: Full-year RPO recovery to $72.4B and cRPO outpacing total RPO (+16% vs +14%) signals near-term demand is accelerating even as the prior Q3 drawdown created noise.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 revenue +9% YoY → FY26 total $41.5B +10% YoY; Agentforce 360 Platform/Slack/Other segment +19% YoY vs core segments +2–9%
shifts.note: Headline growth is steady but the internal mix is bifurcating sharply: AI/consumption segment running at 2x the rate of legacy CRM — watch for mix-shift to lift blended growth in FY27.

shifts.trend: Slack MCP server: 1.2M weekly active external users, 40M weekly tool calls, ~175B data tokens/week within 10 weeks of launch; commercial model 'not finalized'
shifts.note: The engagement metrics are extraordinary but the revenue model is undefined — this is platform optionality that could be a material FY28+ growth layer or a free capability that boosts retention without direct revenue.

shifts.trend: FY26 operating margin 20% (up from 19% FY25); operating cash flow $15.0B (+15% YoY); SBC still high but company repurchased $12.7B of stock in FY26
shifts.note: Capital returns ($12.7B buyback + $1.6B dividend in FY26) demonstrate management's confidence in FCF durability even as AI product investment accelerates.

CRM.verdict: Thesis intact but at an early-monetization inflection that requires patience: Agentforce is generating real AWU volume (1.6B+ in Q1 FY27) and premium ARPU uplift (60–80% on Agentforce editions) but the consumption revenue from AWUs and the Headless 360 commercial model are both undefined, meaning the full revenue impact is deferred into FY27–28. The Informatica $9.6B acquisition adds a data-management layer critical for agent context, but integrating it alongside Agentforce ramp is a resource overhang. Key risk: if Headless 360 remains free infrastructure (driving retention but not direct revenue), the 10% top-line growth ceiling may persist even as the platform's strategic position strengthens.

CRWV.headline: CoreWeave's revenue backlog exploded from $60.7B to $99.4B (+64%) in a single quarter while revenue grew 112% YoY — the purest expression of hyperscaler AI infrastructure demand converting into contracted neocloud revenue.

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $5.1B (+168% YoY); Q4 FY25 implied ~$1.59B → Q1 FY26 $2.08B (+32% QoQ, +112% YoY); FY26 guided $12B-$13B
shifts.note: The FY26 guidance midpoint of $12.5B implies ~145% YoY growth on an already-large $5.1B FY25 base — driven entirely by contracted GPU-cluster capacity coming online, not sales cycle risk.

shifts.trend: $15.1B Dec 2024 → $60.7B Dec 2025 → $98.8B Mar 2026 (+64% QoQ, +6.5x YoY); >$40B new commitments signed in Q1 FY26 alone
shifts.note: A $99B RPO for a company doing $2B per quarter implies ~12 years of forward revenue at current run rates — the financial equivalent of a decade-long supply reservation by the largest AI spenders in the world.

shifts.trend: Microsoft share: 77% of top-2 revenue FY24 → 67% FY25 → 45% Q1 FY26; Meta emerges at 20%; Anthropic and Jane Street ($6B) added
shifts.note: Customer concentration is de-risking faster than expected: Microsoft falling from 77% to 45% while total revenue doubles signals that other frontier AI labs and non-AI customers (Jane Street) are absorbing capacity at least as fast as Microsoft is growing its own commitment.

shifts.trend: $10.3B FY25 → $6.8B Q1 FY26 alone; FY26 guided $31B-$35B (implying ~3x FY25 in a single year)
shifts.note: A $33B midpoint FY26 capex funded almost entirely by debt ($21.6B total debt at 9-15% rates plus Q1 convertible issuance) is a highly leveraged bet on take-or-pay contract certainty — the $99B RPO provides the cash flow visibility to service it.

shifts.trend: Active power: 360 MW (FY24) → 850 MW (FY25) → >1 GW (Q1 FY26); contracted power: 3.1 GW (FY25) → >3.5 GW (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: The 3.5 GW contracted versus 1 GW active means CoreWeave has more than 3x its current active capacity in the buildout pipeline — the conversion of that contracted power into revenue is the primary FY26-FY27 earnings driver.

shifts.trend: Adj EBITDA $1.2B in Q1 FY26 on $2.1B revenue (56% margin); FY26 adj operating income guided $900M-$1.1B
shifts.note: A 56% EBITDA margin at 1 GW active demonstrates the economic model works at scale — the challenge is pure execution: deploying 2.5 GW of additional contracted capacity on schedule while managing a $21.6B debt load.

CRWV.verdict: CoreWeave is the most direct financial read-through of AI inference infrastructure demand: its $99B RPO is a legally contracted forward-order book from the largest AI spenders on earth, growing 64% in a single quarter as Meta, Anthropic, and Jane Street joined Microsoft and OpenAI in locking multi-year GPU capacity. The de-concentration of Microsoft from 67% to 45% of revenue is structurally bullish — it confirms the hyperscaler demand is broad-based, not a single-customer phenomenon. For the inference stack supply chain, CoreWeave's FY26 capex plan of $31-35B (3x its FY25 base) translates directly into sustained demand for GB200/GB300 NVL72 clusters, HBM memory, advanced substrates, power infrastructure, and data-center capacity — making its RPO trajectory one of the most reliable leading indicators of forward silicon and power demand in the entire dashboard.

CSCO.headline: Cisco's AI infrastructure order target nearly doubled mid-year to $9B after 5 hyperscaler design wins in a single quarter — from cautious supply-chain investor to 'networking super cycle' beneficiary in six months.

shifts.trend: Networking product revenue: +15% YoY (Q1 FY26) → +21% YoY (Q2 FY26); AI infrastructure order target $5B → $9B (FY2026, +80% revision in one quarter at Q3)
shifts.note: The velocity of order-target revision — from $5B to $9B in a single quarter — is the most powerful signal that Cisco's hyperscaler positioning with Silicon One has fundamentally shifted.

shifts.trend: Purchase commitments for Silicon One contract manufacturing stepped up Q1 FY26 → Q2 FY26 → Q3 FY26; Cisco made equity investment in Nanya to secure memory supply
shifts.note: Moving upstream into memory equity stakes is an unusual strategic step that signals Cisco views memory supply as a structural constraint to its AI infrastructure growth, not a temporary blip.

shifts.trend: Acacia optics: first called out in Q3 as delivering $1B+ quarterly revenue; 5 hyperscaler design wins in Q3 FY26 (2 optics, 3 systems); pluggable optics TAM framed as equal to switching spend per hyperscaler
shifts.note: Acacia's $1B quarterly optics run-rate arriving alongside optics-specific design wins elevates the segment from incidental to structural — a second growth engine beneath the switching story.

shifts.trend: Product gross margin -0.6pts in Q1 FY26 on mix/pricing → +0.2pts recovery in Q2 FY26; overall margin stable as Silicon One productivity offsets mix headwinds
shifts.note: Cisco's proprietary Silicon One ASIC buffers the margin compression that commodity-switching vendors face — owning the chip is converting from cost control to competitive moat.

shifts.trend: AI infrastructure demand across 4 hyperscaler categories (cloud, sovereign, neocloud, defense) vs. prior 1-2 customer type concentration; 5 franchise slots won at hyperscalers in Q3 alone
shifts.note: Design-win-based hyperscaler franchise slots are durable — once Cisco infrastructure is embedded in a data center topology, displacement requires a full rip-and-replace.

shifts.trend: FY26 AI infrastructure target: $5B (initial) → $9B (Q3 FY26 raise); Splunk security headwinds expected to normalize exiting FY26 into FY27 H1
shifts.note: Security drag from Splunk's ratable transition is masking overall growth; as Splunk compares normalize by FY27 H1, the full AI infrastructure tailwind will be visible in reported totals.

CSCO.verdict: The AI inference thesis for Cisco is materially strengthening: the $9B AI infrastructure target revision, 5 hyperscaler design wins in one quarter, and Acacia's $1B+ optics run-rate confirm that Silicon One is converting from supply-chain differentiator to franchise infrastructure. The key risk is Security/Splunk drag creating deceptively soft headline growth through FY27 H1, masking the networking super-cycle signal. The catalyst is the third scale-across hyperscaler win (announced in Q4 opening weeks) and normalization of Splunk compares, which should unlock multiple expansion for a business that looks cheaper on AI-networking metrics than headline EPS implies.

ETN.headline: Data center orders exploded 240% YoY in Q1, backlog surged to 12-year visibility, and Eaton closed Boyd Thermal to become a chip-to-grid platform play — AI infrastructure demand is now the dominant earnings driver.

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas organic orders: +19% YoY backlog growth at YE25 → +32% organic backlog growth in Q1 FY26; customer orders +42% organic in Q1; data center vertical orders +240% YoY in Q1
shifts.note: The order surge is not just directional improvement — 240% YoY data center orders represents a step-change in demand density that echoes across the backlog math.

shifts.trend: Total company backlog $19.8B at YE25 (+31% YoY for Americas) → Electrical Americas alone $14.5B (+44% YoY, +32% organic) by Q1 FY26; 12-year equivalent cumulative pipeline (228GW) disclosed in Q1
shifts.note: The sequential backlog acceleration from YE25 to Q1 FY26 is particularly notable — multi-year visibility has rarely been this explicit for an industrial name.

shifts.trend: FY25 Electrical Americas +16%; Q1 FY26 Electrical Americas +20% total / +14% organic; Q1 data center vertical +50%; record company revenue $7.5B (+17% total, +10% organic)
shifts.note: Organic growth is reaccelerating into an already-elevated base, suggesting demand is not being pulled forward but is genuinely expanding.

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas operating margin 30.2% in FY24 → 29.9% in FY25 (−30bps) → 25.6% in Q1 FY26 (−480bps YoY); commodity + wage inflation the primary driver
shifts.note: Margin compression is a near-term headwind from cost inflation and capacity investment, not demand weakness; sales leverage of +210bps partially offset, and management expects normalization as pricing catches up.

shifts.trend: YE25: bolt-on acquisitions (Fibrebond, Resilient); Nov 2025: $9.5B Boyd Thermal signed; Q1 FY26: Boyd closed ($1.7B FY26 revenue, backlog doubled in 6 months), NVIDIA Vera Rubin DSX partnership announced
shifts.note: ETN has moved from pure switchgear/PDU provider to a chip-to-grid platform including liquid cooling, solid-state transformers, and next-gen GPU pod infrastructure — TAM expansion is material.

shifts.trend: FY26 organic growth guide raised from 7–9% to 9–11% (+200bps) following Q1 results; adj EPS guide set at $13.05–$13.50
shifts.note: A 200bps raise this early in the year, on top of an already-above-market growth guide, signals management's confidence that AI capex is durable, not front-loaded.

ETN.verdict: The AI infrastructure thesis for ETN is not just intact — it is compounding. Order momentum (data center +240% YoY), backlog trajectory ($14.5B Americas, +44% YoY), and the Boyd Thermal acquisition collectively position ETN as the most complete electrical-to-cooling platform in the power chain. Key risk is near-term margin compression (Electrical Americas −480bps in Q1) from commodity/wage inflation and acquisition integration costs; the bull case depends on pricing power catching up with costs, which management has telegraphed for H2 FY26. The NVIDIA partnership and solid-state transformer pipeline (first orders H2 2026, commercial 2027–28) are long-cycle catalysts that are not yet in numbers.

FN.headline: Fabrinet posted back-to-back record quarters driven by DCI (+90% YoY) and Telecom (+55%) as the 1.6T AI fabric interconnect ramp overwhelmed the Datacom supply constraint — capacity is expanding to $8.5B annualized to serve committed multi-year demand.

shifts.trend: Revenue: $1.13B (+35.9% YoY, Q2 FY26) → $1.21B record (+39.3% YoY, Q3 FY26) → Q4 FY26 guide $1.25B-$1.29B (+~7% QoQ); 9-month revenue $3.33B (+32.5%)
shifts.note: Sequential acceleration in both revenue level and YoY growth rate confirms Fabrinet is absorbing increasing volumes across multiple hyperscaler programs without capacity ceiling emerging — yet.

shifts.trend: DCI revenue: $142.2M Q2 FY26 → $196.9M Q3 FY26 (+90.4% YoY, +38% QoQ); Telecom: $412.2M Q2 FY26 → $431.4M Q3 FY26 (+42.5% YoY, +5% QoQ)
shifts.note: DCI at +90% YoY is the fastest-growing optical line because inter-datacenter AI scale-across interconnect is a new workload category, not share-shifting within an existing market.

shifts.trend: Datacom: $278.1M Q2 FY26 (supply constrained) → $260.4M Q3 FY26 (-6% QoQ, supply still constrained); two new 800G hyperscaler program wins added despite constraint
shifts.note: Datacom declining sequentially while simultaneously winning new hyperscaler programs shows the constraint is purely supply-side — demand is not an issue and new wins will convert as components free up.

shifts.trend: Total manufacturing capacity expanding to $8.5B annualized to serve multi-year committed demand; new ~$133M 2.0M sq-ft Chonburi facility under construction; CapEx $64M in Q3
shifts.note: Committing to $8.5B annualized capacity against a current $4.8B annualized run-rate ($1.2B/qtr × 4) implies management sees demand doubling or more from current levels over the medium term.

shifts.trend: Customers >10% of revenue: 2 customers 48.5% (prior year) → 4 customers 59.1% (Q2 FY26) → 4 customers (Q3 FY26, same dynamic); Nokia/Infinera + Lumentum/NeoPhotonics/Cisco/Acacia consolidation flagged as customer-count risk
shifts.note: Expanding to 4 large customers signals portfolio diversification, but industry consolidation among optical systems vendors means the underlying end-buyer base is narrowing — Fabrinet's customer diversification may be partially illusory.

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 12.2% (Q2 FY26) → 11.9% Q3 FY26 (-30bps QoQ); operating margin: 10.1% Q2 → 9.9% Q3; operating income +52.1% YoY on volume leverage
shifts.note: Thin but stable gross margins are structural for a contract manufacturer — the thesis is volume leverage on operating income, which at +52% YoY is tracking well ahead of the revenue growth rate.

FN.verdict: Fabrinet's positioning as the precision manufacturing backbone for AI optics is intact and strengthening: two record revenue quarters, DCI exploding at 90% YoY, and a $8.5B capacity commitment show the company is locking in the next wave of hyperscaler demand. The investment thesis is less about margin re-rating (GM is structurally thin) and more about sustained volume scaling with operating leverage on the income line. The primary risk is Datacom supply constraint persisting — if 800G component supply frees up, Fabrinet could see a sharp catch-up quarter, but any further delay risks program wins moving to alternative manufacturers. Customer consolidation among optical systems vendors is a secondary risk to watch through 2026-2027.

GEV.headline: Q1 FY26 orders of $18.3B (+71% YoY) hit an all-time record with book-to-bill ~2x, Electrification received more data center orders in one quarter than all of 2025, and GE Vernova pulled forward its $200B backlog target to 2027 — the power generation/grid supply squeeze is tightening, not easing.

shifts.trend: Heavy-duty gas turbine orders: 41 units (2023) → 68 (2024) → 110 (2025) → 28 units in Q1 FY26 alone; total GW orders 8.1GW in Q1 FY26 vs 7.1GW prior year; Q1 FY26 total orders $18.3B (+71% YoY)
shifts.note: The Q1 FY26 order quarter was described as the strongest in company history, and data center customer demand is a growing share of an already-surging Gas Power book.

shifts.trend: Total RPO $119.0B at YE24 → $150.2B at YE25 (+26% YoY) → $163.3B at Q1 FY26 end (+9% sequentially, +32% YoY); Gas Power under contract 83GW at YE25 → 100GW at Q1 FY26 end (+21GW in one quarter)
shifts.note: The sequential RPO jump of $13B in a single quarter, while already at record levels, reflects demand arriving faster than production can absorb — backlog duration is extending, not compressing.

shifts.trend: Electrification data center orders: exceeded all of FY25 in Q1 FY26 alone ($2.4B in Q1 FY26 alone); FY25 saw material growth, Q1 FY26 represents step-change; Gas Power pricing 10–20pts above Q4 FY25 driven by AI/data center demand
shifts.note: The Q1 FY26 Electrification data center order figure ($2.4B in one quarter) is the most concrete quantification of AI demand impact seen in any of GEV's filings.

shifts.trend: Power segment EBITDA margin 12.5% (FY24) → 14.7% (FY25) → 16.3% in Q1 FY26; adj EBITDA $896M in Q1 FY26 (+$439M YoY, +87%); adj EBITDA margin +390bps YoY in Q1
shifts.note: Margin expansion is accelerating as services mix enriches and heavy-duty turbine pricing power compounds — pricing is running 10–20 points above prior quarter levels.

shifts.trend: FY25 FCF $3.7B → Q1 FY26 FCF $4.8B (in one quarter alone, driven by advance payments on long-cycle equipment)
shifts.note: Generating more FCF in Q1 FY26 than in all of FY25 reflects the long-cycle equipment dynamics where customers are paying in advance to secure scarce turbine/transformer delivery slots.

shifts.trend: FY26 initial guide: revenue $44–45B, EBITDA margin 12–14%, FCF $6.5–7.5B → Q1 raise: revenue $44.5–45.5B, same EBITDA margin band, FCF $6.5–7.5B confirmed; $200B backlog target pulled forward from 2028 to 2027
shifts.note: The $200B backlog target pull-forward by one year — announced simultaneously with a record-order quarter — signals management's confidence that Q1 momentum was structural, not episodic.

GEV.verdict: GE Vernova's AI infrastructure thesis is accelerating materially: Gas Power is supply-constrained with pricing power, Electrification is seeing a structural demand surge from data centers ($2.4B in Q1 FY26 alone), and FCF generation is running ahead of full-year guidance after just one quarter. The Prolec transformer acquisition ($5.3B) adds critical manufacturing capacity exactly at a bottleneck point in the grid build-out. Key risks include execution on absorbing a massive order book into fixed turbine/transformer production schedules, tariff exposure ($250M in 2025), and geopolitical risk on international gas power markets; the structural demand driver from AI data center electrification appears durable across multiple delivery cycles.

GNRC.headline: Generac's C&I segment surged 28% to $510M in Q1 FY26 as a $600M hyperscaler notice-to-proceed — the largest single commitment in company history — validated the data center backup power thesis that was still speculative six months earlier.

shifts.trend: Q4 FY25: first non-binding LOI from one hyperscaler; two hyperscalers in vendor approval; Q1 FY26: $600M NTP from one hyperscaler for FY27 deliveries; backlog >$700M (up ~$300M since Feb earnings in 3 months)
shifts.note: The backlog growth of ~$300M in a single quarter — before vendor approvals are even finalized — signals that hyperscalers are willing to commit capital ahead of formal qualification, reflecting supply scarcity of large-format backup generators.

shifts.trend: FY25: data center cited as incremental C&I growth driver; FY25 C&I +4.9% total; Q1 FY26: data center customers cited as primary C&I driver, C&I +28% to $510M; C&I now ~48% of total Q1 revenue
shifts.note: The shift from incremental to primary is the key narrative change — data center backup power has moved from a pilot channel to a structural revenue driver within 12 months.

shifts.trend: FY25 C&I +4.9% (muted by overall residential drag); Q4 FY25 C&I ~25%+; Q1 FY26 C&I +28% to $510M; FY26 C&I guide raised from low-to-mid teens to mid-to-high 20% growth
shifts.note: C&I growth has moved from low single-digit to high-twenties percent within a year — a category-level inflection driven by a new product cycle (large-megawatt diesel generators) meeting a new demand category (hyperscale data centers).

shifts.trend: FY25 adj EBITDA margin 17.0% (vs 18.4% in FY24) on residential deleverage; Q1 FY25 adj EBITDA margin 15.9% → Q1 FY26 adj EBITDA margin 18.3% (+240bps); C&I segment margin 11.4% (Q1 FY25) → 13.0% (Q1 FY26) on price/cost + Allmand accretion
shifts.note: The C&I mix shift toward higher-margin large-megawatt generators is driving visible margin recovery — the Q1 FY26 adj EBITDA margin of 18.3% already matches the top end of prior-year annual performance despite residential softness.

shifts.trend: FY25: large-megawatt diesel generator product launch; Sussex, WI expansion announced targeting >$1B domestic C&I capacity; Q1 FY26: Allmand ($123M) and Enercon ($122M) acquisitions closed; Sussex on track for H2 2026
shifts.note: Sequential capacity investments across organic (Sussex facility) and inorganic (two acquisitions) channels suggest Generac is converting LOIs into committed demand and building ahead of formal hyperscaler vendor approvals.

shifts.trend: Large-megawatt backup generator market described as 'significantly supply-constrained' in FY25 10-K; hyperscaler NTP received ahead of vendor approval completion; Sussex expansion targeted to address capacity gap
shifts.note: Generac receiving a $600M NTP before formal vendor approval is complete — an unusual sequencing — underscores how supply-constrained the large-format backup power market is, giving Generac pricing leverage and long-cycle visibility.

GNRC.verdict: Generac's data center thesis crossed a validation threshold in Q1 FY26: the $600M hyperscaler NTP, backlog growth of $300M in three months, and C&I guidance raise to mid-to-high 20% growth confirm that the large-megawatt backup power opportunity is real and accelerating. The competitive moat is supply scarcity (market is self-described as supply-constrained) plus Generac's exclusive engine supplier agreement and domestic manufacturing scale (Sussex facility). Key risk is that vendor approval completion — described as '99 yards of 100-yard dash' — still has not converted to firm purchase orders, and a hyperscaler procurement pause could defer the FY27 backlog into later years; residential segment softness also continues to weigh on total-company optics.

GOOGL.headline: Google Cloud accelerated to 63% growth and $20B quarterly revenue while Alphabet raised 2026 capex to $180-190B and disclosed multi-gigawatt TPU hardware supply deals — the most aggressive infrastructure posture of any hyperscaler.

shifts.trend: $52.5B FY24 → $91.4B FY25 (+74%) → $35.7B Q1 FY26 alone; FY26 guidance raised to $180B-$190B; FY27 to 'significantly increase' further
shifts.note: The Q1 FY26 capex ($35.7B) is already 39% of the entire FY25 spend in a single quarter; the multi-year escalation trajectory — $52.5B → $91.4B → ~$185B — is the steepest of the major hyperscalers and signals structural, not cyclical, infrastructure investment.

shifts.trend: Google Cloud $43.1B FY24 → $58.7B FY25 (+36%) → $20.0B Q1 FY26 (+63% YoY)
shifts.note: The acceleration from 36% annual growth to 63% in Q1 FY26 is driven by AI workloads where GCP is growing faster than Cloud overall; management explicitly stated Cloud would have been higher if not compute-constrained.

shifts.trend: Cloud operating margin 10.4% FY25 → 32.9% Q1 FY26 (vs 17.8% Q1 FY25), with op income $6.6B vs $2.2B (+3x YoY)
shifts.note: Tripling of Cloud operating income in a quarter where capex hit $35.7B is counterintuitive — it reflects long-lived depreciation cycles and scale benefits; the margin trajectory supports sustained aggressive investment.

shifts.trend: $242.8B at Dec 31 2025 → $467.6B at Mar 31 2026 (+93% in one quarter); $462.3B is Google Cloud
shifts.note: The near-doubling of RPO in a single quarter — a $225B increase — is extraordinary and reflects the closing of several large multi-year AI infrastructure contracts; the scale implies Google Cloud is competing and winning against AWS and Azure for hyperscale AI commitments.

shifts.trend: GenAI model revenue +~800% YoY in Cloud; first-party models processing >16B tokens/minute (up from 10B last quarter); Google AI Overviews running at billions of queries
shifts.note: Token throughput growth of 60% quarter-on-quarter at Google's own first-party inference scale is the clearest demand-pull signal for TPU silicon, networking, and power infrastructure in any hyperscaler disclosure.

shifts.trend: First-time disclosure of 'multi-gigawatt' TPU hardware supply agreements to external customers; revenues start late 2026 with majority in 2027
shifts.note: Selling multi-gigawatt TPU capacity externally — with revenue booked into the $467.6B RPO — represents a new model where Google's silicon supply chain (substrates, HBM, packaging) must scale to serve third-party AI infrastructure demand, not just internal workloads.

GOOGL.verdict: Alphabet's infrastructure ramp is the most aggressive in the hyperscaler cohort by capex growth rate: FY25 spend of $91.4B is already up 74% YoY and the 2026 guidance midpoint of $185B implies another ~100% step-up, with FY27 explicitly guided to increase further still. Google Cloud's acceleration to 63% growth and near-doubling of RPO in Q1 FY26 confirm that AI workload demand is absorbing capacity faster than it can be built — management's own admission that Cloud would have been higher if not compute-constrained is the strongest supply-tightness signal across the hyperscaler earnings cycle. For the inference stack, Alphabet's multi-gigawatt TPU supply deals introduce a new demand vector: external customers committing to Google-built AI infrastructure, pulling through HBM, advanced packaging, power, and data-center construction at a pace that sustains the capex escalation through at least 2028.

HPE.covers: FY25 10-K → Q1 FY26 → Q2 FY26 → FY26 guidance framework (Dec 18, 2025 – Jun 5, 2026)
HPE.headline: HPE's networking inflection from Juniper is real and now confirmed — triple-digit traditional server order growth in Q2 and a $5B AI backlog show both the compute and switching legs of the AI infrastructure thesis are accelerating simultaneously.

shifts.trend: FY25 Networking +51.1% YoY ($2.3B incremental, majority from Juniper 4-month contribution) → Q1 FY26 Networking +151.5% YoY ($2.7B, full-quarter Juniper) → Q2 FY26 Networking +~10% normalized → FY26 full-year guided +8%-12% normalized (reported +68%-73%)
shifts.note: Once Juniper laps its acquisition date in Q4 FY26, the normalized growth rate of 8-12% is the clean signal; data center switching orders +mid-40% YoY and routing +mid-20% YoY confirm organic momentum.

shifts.trend: FY25: 'significant AI backlog' flagged qualitatively → Q1 FY26: AI Systems backlog record $5B → pipeline described as 'multiples of backlog'
shifts.note: A $5B AI backlog against a ~$6B quarterly Cloud & AI segment run rate implies roughly two quarters of AI server revenue already contracted — providing durable revenue visibility into late FY26.

shifts.trend: FY25: server +10.2% YoY on AUPs → Q1 FY26: AI server growing but total Cloud & AI -2.7% on normalized basis → Q2 FY26: traditional server revenue +33% YoY, orders triple-digit YoY; ASPs substantially higher
shifts.note: Triple-digit order growth in Q2 is the strongest server demand signal HPE has reported in years; management explicitly stated this is not pull-in activity, reinforcing sustainable demand confidence.

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 cumulative FY26 target $1.7B-$1.9B → Q2 FY26 target raised to $2.0B
shifts.note: The $2B Networks for AI cumulative order target for FY26 reflects hyperscaler data center switching and enterprise campus/branch as separate, concurrent tailwinds — not a single concentrated bet.

shifts.trend: FY25 FCF pressured by Juniper acquisition → Q1 FY26 FCF $708M → Q2 FY26 FCF $915M (up $1.8B YoY); net leverage 2x target pulled forward to end of FY26 from end of FY27
shifts.note: FCF recovery one full year ahead of plan unlocks 75% shareholder return policy in early FY27, making HPE a potential capital-return catalyst as well as a cycle play.

shifts.trend: FY25 non-GAAP operating margin 9.8% (-0.7pts) → Q1 FY26 non-GAAP OM 12.7% (+280bps) → Networking OM Q2 ~21%; guided mid-to-high 20s in FY27 on Juniper synergies
shifts.note: Juniper is a structurally higher-margin business — as $600M synergies flow through by FY28, blended operating margin expansion of 200-300bps is plausible even if AI server gross margins remain limited.

HPE.verdict: HPE's thesis is intact and gaining breadth — Juniper networking is inflecting organically (not just a lapping effect), AI server backlog at $5B and triple-digit order growth in Q2 confirm genuine demand, and FCF improvement ahead of schedule reduces balance-sheet risk from the $13.4B Juniper acquisition. The key risk is margin sustainability: GPU-heavy AI server deals carry limited margins (management's own language), DRAM costs tripled QoQ in Q1, and if AI server AUPs compress as supply normalizes, HPE's Cloud & AI segment margin could deteriorate faster than Juniper synergies can compensate.

HUBB.headline: Hubbell's data center electrical products growth nearly doubled from ~25% in Q4 FY25 to ~40% in Q1 FY26, triggering a full-year data center growth outlook raise from 15% to >25% — the intersection of AI infrastructure and grid modernization is Hubbell's most powerful simultaneous tailwind in years.

shifts.trend: FY25: data center primary driver of Electrical Solutions +7.1% organic growth; Q4 FY25: data center end market +~25%; Q1 FY26: data center end market +~40% (well above prior expectations); FY26 data center guide raised from 15% to >25%
shifts.note: The guidance raise — from 15% to >25% on a whole-year basis after just one quarter — indicates the AI infrastructure buildout is hitting Hubbell's product set (wiring devices, connectors, switchgear) with accelerating velocity.

shifts.trend: FY25 organic growth +3.3%; Q1 FY26 organic +8.2% total (Electrical Solutions +10.6% organic, Utility Solutions +6.8% organic); FY26 guide raised from organic +4–7% to +6–9%
shifts.note: Organic growth more than doubled sequentially from FY25 to Q1 FY26, driven by convergent tailwinds from data center electrical products and grid T&D infrastructure — two independent AI-linked demand streams.

shifts.trend: Total firm backlog $1.90B at YE24 → $2.16B at YE25 (+14%) → substantially all targeted for shipment in 2026; continued backlog growth implied by Q1 data center and utility T&D order strength
shifts.note: The backlog composition shift toward data center (higher-margin) and 765kV transmission (longer-cycle) products implies improving revenue quality alongside volume growth.

shifts.trend: FY25 adj operating margin +80bps to 22.5%; Q1 FY26 consolidated adj operating margin +110bps; Electrical Solutions adj margin −30bps to 16.4% (tariff offset by volume); Utility Solutions adj margin +190bps to 21.8%
shifts.note: Margin dynamics are bifurcated — Electrical Solutions facing tariff/commodity headwinds while Utility Solutions expands on T&D mix; the net effect at the consolidated level is still positive, with more upside if tariff headwinds subside.

shifts.trend: FY25: $958M in acquisitions including DMC Power (high-current connectors for AI compute); Q1 FY26: DMC performing above expectations, contributing to data center outperformance; 765kV transmission identified as $1.5B incremental TAM over 10 years
shifts.note: Hubbell's acquisition of DMC Power (connectors for AI compute clusters, including direct liquid cooling interconnects) is the most targeted AI infrastructure bet in the portfolio and is already delivering above underwriting.

shifts.trend: FY26 initial guide (Feb 2026): net sales +5–8%, organic +4–7%, adj EPS $18.50–$19.00 → post-Q1 raise: net sales +8–11%, organic +6–9%, adj EPS $19.30–$19.85; data center growth >25% for full year
shifts.note: Guidance raised across every metric after Q1, with the data center growth revision (15% → >25%) being the most strategically significant signal.

HUBB.verdict: Hubbell's dual exposure to data center electrical infrastructure (Electrical Solutions) and AI-driven grid modernization (Utility Solutions, 765kV transmission) is creating the strongest revenue growth environment in years — Q1 FY26 organic growth of 8%+ represented a meaningful acceleration from the FY25 pace. The DMC Power acquisition is proving prescient, and the 765kV transmission opportunity ($1.5B incremental TAM) provides a long-cycle utility revenue stream orthogonal to data center capex cycles. Key risks are modest: Aclara grid automation softness (expected to stabilize in Q2 FY26) and commodity/tariff headwinds compressing Electrical Solutions margins; both are manageable given the >25% data center growth guide and the company's domestic manufacturing base (broadly tariff-neutral per management).

INTC.covers: Q4 FY25 (8-K/implied) → FY25 10-K → Q1 FY26 10-Q → Q1 FY26 8-K (Jan 22 – Apr 24, 2026)
INTC.headline: Intel is beating guidance for the sixth straight quarter as demand outpaces supply — 18A yields ahead of schedule and ASIC at $1B+ run rate show Lip-Bu Tan's foundry-and-AI turnaround has traction, but losses remain deep.

shifts.trend: FY25 DCAI $14.3B (+794M YoY, ~6%) → Q4 FY25 DCAI ~$4.8B (implied sequential) → Q1 FY26 DCAI $5.1B (+22% YoY, +7% QoQ) → Q2 FY26 DCAI guided up double-digits sequentially
shifts.note: Server CPU ASPs rose 27% YoY in Q1 FY26 as premium Xeon 6 mix took hold; even with -5% volume due to supply constraints, revenue grew 22% — demand is real and supply-constrained.

shifts.trend: FY25: 18A ramped to high-volume production with internal products → Q1 FY26: 18A yields running ahead of internal year-end targets; Panther Lake volume +6-7x QoQ → Intel 14A PDK 0.9 in development with multiple external customers
shifts.note: Yield outperformance is the single most important turnaround datapoint — if 18A yields hold at or above internal target through H2 2026, external foundry revenue should begin materializing in FY27.

shifts.trend: FY25: ASIC nascent → Q1 FY26 ASIC revenue >$1B annualized run rate (+30% QoQ, nearly doubling YoY); Google signed multi-year Xeon IPU agreement; Xeon selected as host CPU for NVIDIA DGX Rubin NVL8
shifts.note: ASIC revenue growing faster than any other Intel segment and provides a bridge to foundry wins — a customer that buys an Intel-fabbed ASIC is one step from being an external foundry customer.

shifts.trend: Q4 FY25 = 5th consecutive beat → Q1 FY26 revenue $13.6B ($1.4B above midpoint) = 6th consecutive beat; non-GAAP GM 41% (~650bps above guidance
shifts.note: Consistent upside delivery is restoring management credibility after years of missed targets; gross margin beats reflect better-than-expected 18A economics plus premium mix benefits.

shifts.trend: FY25 Foundry operating loss $10.3B (external revenue only $307M) → Q1 FY26 Foundry operating loss $2.4B (vs $2.3B Q1 2025) with external revenue $174M
shifts.note: Losses are not yet shrinking despite revenue growth — 18A early-ramp wafer costs are elevated, and $4.1B of Q1 restructuring charges inflated GAAP losses to $3.7B net; the path to foundry profitability requires external customer wins beyond the current ~$174M/quarter.

shifts.trend: Intel 7 supply constraints flagged at Q4 FY25 → Q1 FY26: demand exceeded supply across all segments; external shortages (substrates, memory) expected to persist through H1 2026
shifts.note: Ironically, supply scarcity is supporting premium ASP and pricing discipline — but volume recovery in H2 2026 is required to prove the thesis that demand is durable, not just constrained.

INTC.verdict: Intel's turnaround is real but fragile — six beats in a row, 18A yields ahead of plan, and a $1B ASIC run rate are all genuine positives, but the Foundry segment still burns $2.4B/quarter with only $174M in external revenue to show for it, and the 14A roadmap explicitly depends on securing a significant external customer. The thesis is intact as a foundry recovery story with a 2-3 year horizon; the near-term risk is that supply constraints cap revenue upside in H1 2026, and the medium-term risk is that 14A gets paused if external traction doesn't materialise, forcing a costly pivot back to TSMC dependency.

KLAC.headline: KLA is delivering record results across process control — Q3 FY26 revenue $3.42B (+11% YoY) with advanced packaging process control hitting ~$1B in 2026 (up from $635M in 2025) and management unprecedented in guiding 2027 WFE growth above 2026 rates in April.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 $3.30B (+7% YoY) → Q3 FY26 $3.42B (+11% YoY, record quarter) → Q4 FY26 guided $3.575B midpoint (implied +13%+ YoY)
shifts.note: Sequential acceleration in YoY growth rate across consecutive quarters confirms WFE spending is broadening from early-cycle memory into foundry/logic alongside continued DRAM/HBM investment.

shifts.trend: $635M in CY2025 → guided ~$1B in CY2026 (high-50s% growth); KLA gained 14 percentage points of share and reached #1 position for advanced wafer-level packaging process control in 2025
shifts.note: CoWoS and SoIC hybrid-bonding inspection driving the ramp; as AI infrastructure spending drives more chips into 2.5D/3D packages, KLAC's advanced packaging revenue becomes a durable structural growth vector.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 Korea +34%/NA +38% (memory-led); Q3 FY26 Korea +80% YoY while Taiwan -12% on timing; nine-month China revenue flat as US export controls constrain
shifts.note: Memory (DRAM/HBM-led) is accelerating faster than foundry within the quarter; Korea dominance in memory ramp is shifting KLAC's geographic mix favorably toward higher-ASP leading-edge process control.

shifts.trend: WFE guidance progressed from 'solid growth' in FY25 → 'exceeds $140B in 2026' (Q3 FY26 call) → 2027 WFE growth rate expected to EXCEED 2026 rate (unprecedented early visibility in April)
shifts.note: KLAC's early 2027 guidance reflects 8-quarter rolling customer demand forecasts — this is structural supply-chain signaling, not optimism; a $215B ± $20B WFE market by 2030 underpins the 13-17% CAGR investor-day model.

shifts.trend: Services Q2 FY26 $786M (+18% YoY) → Q3 FY26 $775M (+16% YoY); long-term CAGR model raised to 13-15% at March 2026 Investor Day; 16th and 17th consecutive annual dividend increases
shifts.note: Services now 23-24% of revenue and growing above the company average, creating a stable earnings base against any future WFE cyclicality — dividend consistency reflects management confidence in this baseline.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 61.4% (+1.1pts YoY) → Q3 FY26 62.2% → Q4 FY26 guided 61.75%; ~100bp DRAM chip cost headwind on image processing computers persisting through 2026
shifts.note: Gross margin is holding in the 61-62% band despite structural DRAM input cost headwind; management frames this as transitory, and product mix improvement toward leading-edge process control is the offset.

KLAC.verdict: Thesis intact at high conviction: KLA is the dominant process control platform with ~7x scale advantage over nearest competitors, gaining share in the fastest-growing WFE sub-segments (advanced packaging +high-50s%, DRAM/HBM) while its services franchise compounds independently of equipment cycles. The unprompted April 2027 guidance raise is the clearest demand-signal in the dataset — managements with 8-quarter rolling forecasts from TSMC, Samsung, Micron, and SK Hynix rarely signal growth acceleration without visibility. Key risk is the DRAM input cost headwind to gross margins persisting longer than guided, and any China export-control escalation that impacts the ~24% China revenue base.

LEU.headline: Centrus landed the largest US nuclear enrichment contract in recent history ($900M HALEU DOE award), raised guidance twice in three months, and formed a JV with Oklo that would make Piketon the end-to-end HALEU hub for advanced reactors.

shifts.trend: Backlog $3.8B at YE25 (vs $3.7B YE24) with $2.3B contingent LEU → $3.9B at Q1 FY26 with $2.4B contingent LEU + $0.8B CTS; $900M HALEU DOE task order added (Jan 2026)
shifts.note: The $900M HALEU award — largest enrichment government contract in recent US history — converted a regulatory ambition into concrete funded work; the contingent LEU backlog growing to $2.4B shows utility demand firming as non-Russian supply capacity is announced.

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue ~$430–440M (implied) → FY26 initial guide $425–475M (Feb 2026) → raised to $450–500M (May 2026) after DOE award; Q1 FY26 revenue $76.7M (+5% YoY)
shifts.note: Two upward guidance revisions in three months reflects both the DOE HALEU contract revenue recognition and the Technical Solutions segment (+47% YoY Q1 FY26) ramping faster than expected.

shifts.trend: SWU spot prices at $34 historic low in 2018 → +52% YoY in FY25 → spot $200 at Q1 FY26 quarter-end (+488% vs 2018 trough); SWU costs +45% YoY — spread stable and widening
shifts.note: European utility de-Russification drove the structural repricing; with Russia representing 43% of global capacity and non-Russian market structurally undersupplied, the pricing tailwind is durable rather than cyclical.

shifts.trend: 900 kg HALEU delivered under Phase 2 (by Jun 2025) → >1.6 metric tons cumulative HALEU (by Q1 FY26); Oak Ridge centrifuge manufacturing started Dec 2025 ($560M+ investment); Fluor named EPC for Piketon LEU+HALEU expansion (Feb 2026)
shifts.note: The manufacturing flywheel is turning: domestic centrifuges ordered, EPC contracted, and Piketon hiring target doubled from 50+ to 100+ workers — capacity will follow the demand signal.

shifts.trend: Sole NRC-licensed HALEU enricher (static moat) → $62.4M §48C tax credit (Jan 2025) → Palantir AI partnership identified ~$300M potential cost savings (Q1 FY26) → Oklo JV exploration for HALEU deconversion at Piketon (Mar 2026)
shifts.note: The Oklo JV, co-located adjacent to Oklo's planned 1.2 GW Pike County power campus, would make Centrus the fuel supplier to the largest advanced reactor deployment in the US — a vertically integrated nuclear fuel-to-power position.

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 net income $10.0M vs $27.2M prior year; advanced technology costs $18.9M vs $3.0M (+530%) from Piketon/Oak Ridge expansion
shifts.note: Near-term earnings are being sacrificed for capacity investment; the $805M 0% convertible (Aug 2025) and early redemption of the 8.25% notes (Mar 2025) have cleaned up the balance sheet, but the $1.9B cash pile needs to be deployed carefully against a DOE funding gap risk.

LEU.verdict: The enrichment moat thesis is strengthening: Centrus is the only Western entity capable of producing HALEU at commercial scale, SWU prices have repriced structurally, and the DOE award provides a funded growth ramp through Piketon expansion. The key risk is Congressional appropriations — the FY27 DOE budget proposal does not include HALEU Operation Contract cascade funding, meaning the outer-year HALEU revenue (and the contingent $2.4B backlog conversion) depends on future budget cycles. The Oklo JV and hyperscaler-adjacent power-campus positioning reduce demand-side risk, but supply-chain funding dependence on federal appropriations remains the thesis's main vulnerability.

LITE.headline: Lumentum's revenue nearly doubled YoY in back-to-back quarters as NVIDIA's $2B equity investment locked in the EML/InP supply chain — but a >30% persistent supply-demand imbalance means the bottleneck is structural, not seasonal.

shifts.note: Acceleration from 65% to 90% YoY in a single quarter — with Q4 guide implying another ~19% sequential step — shows the 1.6T transceiver ramp is still in early innings.

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 36.1% Q2 FY26 (+1,130bps YoY) → 44.2% Q3 FY26 (+1,540bps YoY); non-GAAP GM: ~42.4% Q2 → 47.9% Q3; Q4 guide implies non-GAAP op margin 35%-36%
