shifts.note: Over 1,500bps of gross margin expansion in two quarters driven by 200G-lane mix shift and supply-allocation pricing power — this is the re-rating event the thesis anticipated.

shifts.trend: EML supply-demand imbalance: flagged at >30% in Q2 FY26, persisted at >30% in Q3 FY26; OCS ramp still capacity-constrained; capex $125M in Q3 alone for EML and OCS expansion
shifts.note: A >30% sustained imbalance lasting two quarters with no sign of relief validates LITE as the structural InP/EML choke point for the entire 1.6T transceiver ecosystem.

shifts.trend: NVIDIA strategic equity investment $2.02B received in Q3 FY26; cash balance increased to $3.17B; Q3 capex $125M; additional FY27 capacity expansion planned
shifts.note: A $2B direct equity stake from NVIDIA is not just financing — it is supply chain lock-in that makes Lumentum a captive supplier for the world's dominant AI accelerator vendor.

shifts.trend: Top-2 customer concentration: 24%+17% (Q2 FY26) → 26%+12% (Q3 FY26, combined ~38%); top customer's share rising
shifts.note: Rising concentration toward a single dominant customer (likely tied to NVIDIA ecosystem demand) increases event risk — a single program delay could create sharp quarterly revenue volatility.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 guidance: ~$808M → actual $808.4M (in-line beat); Q4 FY26 guide $960M-$1.01B represents +19% to +25% sequential growth
shifts.note: Two consecutive in-line-to-beat guide/execute cycles with a substantially raised Q4 step-up confirm that supply additions are translating directly to recognizable revenue.

LITE.verdict: Lumentum is the clearest structural bottleneck play in the inference optics stack: NVIDIA's $2B equity investment is an explicit acknowledgment that EML/InP supply is the binding constraint on 1.6T transceiver deployment, and Lumentum holds the only volume-scale InP capability. The thesis is not just intact — it is strengthening with each quarter. The primary risk is customer concentration: the top customer's share rose sequentially to 26%, and a program push-out or technology substitution (CPO direct-drive bypassing EML) in 2027-2028 could create a sharp demand cliff after the current hypercycle peaks.

LRCX.headline: Lam Research is posting three consecutive record revenue quarters with Q3 FY26 at $5.84B (+24% YoY), WFE raised to $140B+ with 2027 growth expected above 2026, and CSBG crossing $2B for the first time — the NAND conversion cycle pull-forward adds a second major upside driver beyond HBM.

shifts.trend: H1 FY26 (six months) $10.67B vs $8.54B prior year (+25%) → Q3 FY26 $5.84B (+24% YoY, +9% QoQ) → Q4 FY26 guided $6.6B midpoint (+13% QoQ implied)
shifts.note: Three consecutive record quarters underscore broad-based WFE demand rather than a lumpy systems shipment; the Q4 guide to $6.6B represents the highest-ever Lam quarterly revenue if achieved.

shifts.trend: DRAM as % of systems revenue: Q2 FY26 23% → Q3 FY26 27% (record); nine-month memory mix 39% (from 34% prior year); LRCX Striker carbide ALD tools at record at all leading DRAM makers
shifts.note: The 1c-node DRAM transition requires ALD silicon-carbide low-k dielectrics — a LRCX-specific tool of record position — creating incremental revenue per wafer start beyond capacity additions alone.

shifts.trend: ~$40B industry upgrade cycle to convert installed base to >200-layer; originally multi-year → management pulled forward to 'majority of spending before end of CY2027'; NAND 12% of Q3 systems revenue, up from 11% in Q2
shifts.note: Data-center NAND bits expected to exceed both PC and mobile combined in 2026 — AI storage demand is compressing the NAND conversion timeline and adding a second structural growth vector alongside HBM for Lam.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 CSBG approaching $2B → Q3 FY26 CSBG $2.11B record (+25% YoY); customer-support revenue $2.11B (+6% QoQ); Dextro cobot expanded to 8 tool types
shifts.note: CSBG crossing $2B is a structural milestone; at $2.1B/quarter the services business alone generates ~$8.4B annualized revenue with lower cyclicality than systems — reducing earnings volatility through cycle troughs.

shifts.trend: $135B initial FY26 WFE estimate (Q2 FY26 call) → raised to $140B+ with bias to upside (Q3 FY26 call); 2027 WFE growth rate expected to exceed 2026 growth rate
shifts.note: Lam's SAM as % of WFE expanding from mid-30s% to slightly above mid-30s% in 2026 and toward high-30s% — every WFE dollar increase disproportionately accrues to Lam as SAM share expands.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 49.6% → Q3 FY26 49.8-49.9% → Q4 FY26 guided 50.5% ± 100bp; six-month GM 50.0% vs 47.7% prior year
shifts.note: Gross margin is grinding higher toward 50.5% as mix improves; near-term pressure from China customer mix (35% of revenue in Q3) constrains the pace, but Q4 guide is above 50% for the first time at scale.

LRCX.verdict: Thesis intact with upside broadening: LRCX entered 2026 as a DRAM/HBM play and is now seeing the NAND conversion cycle pull-forward add a second demand driver simultaneously — both structural (1c-node ALD tools, >200-layer NAND upgrades) and cyclical (industry utilization at record highs boosting CSBG). The $6.6B Q4 guide to new record revenue with margin expansion confirms management visibility. Key risks are China (~34% of Q3 revenue) export-control tightening and tariff-driven cost headwinds on gross margin; both are flagged by management but offset by geographic diversification toward Korea and Taiwan gaining share.

META.headline: Meta raised 2026 capex guidance twice in four months — from $115-135B to $125-145B — while delivering 33% revenue growth and 41% operating margin, making it the highest-conviction demand signal for AI infrastructure.

shifts.trend: $39.2B FY24 → $72.2B FY25 → $19.8B Q1 FY26 alone; FY26 guidance raised to $125B-$145B (from $115B-$135B at year-end)
shifts.note: The in-quarter guidance raise was attributed explicitly to higher memory component costs and additional data center commitments — the first filed acknowledgment that DRAM/HBM pricing is directly inflating hyperscaler capex budgets.

shifts.trend: Total revenue $164.5B FY24 → $201.0B FY25 (+22%) → $56.3B Q1 FY26 (+33% YoY, +29% constant currency)
shifts.note: Revenue growth re-accelerated four points sequentially on rising ad impression volume (+19%) and price (+12%), validating that AI-driven ad personalization is converting capex into measurable top-line returns.

shifts.trend: FY25 Family of Apps operating income $102.5B (+18%); Q1 FY26 group operating margin 41%
shifts.note: 41% operating margin while running $19.8B of quarterly capex demonstrates that Meta's AI ad ROI loop is intact and gives management cover to sustain the $125-145B FY26 build.

shifts.trend: Non-cancelable contractual commitments stepped up $107B in Q1 alone to $237.7B; off-balance-sheet leases not yet commenced = $182.9B
shifts.note: The $237.7B in committed spend — covering servers, third-party cloud capacity, and data centers through 2036 — locks in years of pull-through demand for power, cooling, networking, and silicon layers.

shifts.trend: Rolling out >1 GW of Broadcom-designed custom silicon; also deploying AMD chips alongside new NVIDIA systems
shifts.note: A >1 GW custom-silicon deployment is the largest disclosed non-NVIDIA AI chip rollout by any hyperscaler, signaling that Broadcom's ASIC franchise and Meta's power/rack supply chains are operating at a scale that rivals NVIDIA's NVL deployments.

shifts.trend: Business AI weekly conversations 10x since start of 2026 to 10M/week; Meta AI user sessions per-user up double-digits following Muse Spark
shifts.note: 10x conversation volume growth in a single quarter confirms that AI inference workloads — not just training — are becoming the dominant capex driver, directly benefiting inference-stack supply-chain layers.

META.verdict: Meta is the clearest example of the AI capex-to-revenue flywheel closing: 33% revenue growth at 41% operating margin funds a $125-145B FY26 infrastructure build that is already pulling through >1 GW of custom silicon and $237.7B in contractual commitments. The in-quarter capex raise, explicitly tied to memory pricing, is the most direct evidence from any hyperscaler that HBM/DRAM supply tightness is feeding into declared spending plans. For the inference stack, Meta represents multi-year locked-in demand across GPU, custom silicon (ASIC), power, cooling, and data-center capacity — with commitments extending to 2036 already on the books.

MOD.headline: Modine's data center cooling revenue went from +24% to +158% in a single fiscal year with a $4B LTA signed for 2027–2029, and the company is reshaping into a pure-play cooling platform by spinning off Performance Technologies.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26: data center +$67M YoY (Climate Solutions +24%) → Q3 FY26: data center +$130M, segment +51%, full-year DC guide raised to >70% → Q4 FY26: data center $400M+ (+158% YoY); FY26 total DC $1.1B (+73%)
shifts.note: The quarterly growth rate went from mid-double-digits to triple digits within two quarters of the fiscal year — the Q4 FY26 +158% print reflects new chiller line commissioning finally converting the capex investment into revenue.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26: $100M incremental capex announced; CapEx $59.4M in 1H → Q3 FY26: 4 new chiller lines commissioned (Jefferson City MO); Q4 FY26 4 more lines (Grenada MS, Dallas TX); North American chiller production fivefold increase in FY26; new 3MW chiller announced (50% more capacity, 9% larger footprint)
shifts.note: Modine converted the capex commitment to physical chiller lines in three to four months; the fivefold capacity increase confirms that supply, not demand, was the binding constraint through most of FY26.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26: LTA negotiations in advanced stages → Q3 FY26: record order intake, LTA closings expected → Q4 FY26: $4B LTA signed for 2027–2029 with $165M upfront cash — first LTA of this scale in company history
shifts.note: The $4B LTA (three-year forward commitment from a hyperscaler) is transformative: it provides $1.3–1.4B of annual revenue visibility for three years and the upfront cash payment signals the hyperscaler's urgency to lock in cooling supply ahead of AI infrastructure scale-up.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26: 13.7% operating margin (down from 17.6%, pressured by capacity ramp inefficiencies) → Q3 FY26: 15.3% operating margin (sequential improvement, still below prior levels) → Q4 FY26: guided 20–21% EBITDA margin as new lines reach efficiency
shifts.note: The margin trajectory is a classic manufacturing scale-up J-curve: compressed during rapid line additions, recovering as new lines hit utilization; Q4 exit rate at 20–21% EBITDA would be a new record for the segment.

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26: Performance Technologies contributing ~35% of revenue → Q3 FY26: PT spin-off/merger with Gentherm announced (Jan 2026, ~$1B valuation, $210M cash to MOD) → FY27: Modine becomes pure-play data center cooling + commercial HVAC/refrigeration company
shifts.note: The PT divestiture removes a -4% revenue drag, focuses management attention entirely on cooling, and provides $210M of cash that can be redeployed into the $4B LTA capacity ramp — the strategic logic is straightforward.

shifts.trend: FY26 data center $1.1B (vs ~$635M in FY25, +73%) → FY27 guidance: data center +60–80% (implied ~$1.8–2.0B); total company +20–35%; adj EBITDA $650–680M (+40%+); FCF 4–6% of revenue
shifts.note: Management's original $2B-by-FY28 target is now expected to be achieved one year early in FY27, with the $4B LTA providing the contractual underpin for the growth guidance.

MOD.verdict: Modine's data center cooling thesis has inflected from a growth story to a contracted, cash-generative platform: the $4B LTA, upfront cash, and fivefold chiller capacity build confirm that cooling is now a strategic supply-chain priority for hyperscalers, not a spot-market commodity. The PT spin-off removes a distraction and allows the balance sheet to be fully focused on the data center CAGR. Key risks are execution on margin recovery in FY27 (Q2 and Q3 FY26 margins were below prior levels due to ramp inefficiencies) and the single-LTA concentration risk — if the counterparty pauses AI data center buildout, the $4B contracted backlog could be renegotiated. Leverage at 0.8x net debt at FY26 year-end is manageable, but the company will need to fund the FY27 capacity build from the LTA cash and operating cash flow simultaneously.

MRVL.covers: Q3 FY26 → Q4 FY26/FY26 10-K → Q1 FY27 → Q2 FY27 guidance (Dec 3, 2025 – May 28, 2026)
MRVL.headline: Marvell's data center is transforming from a networking chip vendor into a hyperscale platform — custom silicon to double in FY28, interconnect >70% YoY, and a new NVIDIA partnership expands TAM further.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 DC $1.52B (+38% YoY, 73% mix) → Q4 FY26 DC $1.65B (+21% YoY) → Q1 FY27 DC $1.83B (+27% YoY, 76% mix) → Q2 FY27 DC guided mid-to-high-teens sequential (+mid-40% YoY)
shifts.note: Sequential acceleration is re-establishing after a brief Q4 dip, driven by custom silicon milestones and interconnect product cycle inflections.
Back-to-back upward revisions within six months confirm demand is exceeding internal supply-planning assumptions, not just seasonal mix.

shifts.trend: FY25 custom ~$750M → FY26 custom doubled to ~$1.5B → FY27 guided +>20% → FY28 guided to 'more than double' again → FY29 target >$10B
shifts.note: Three new Tier 1 XPU programmes (lead customer growing, new customer volume ramp in FY28, 10+ attach programmes) underpin confidence in the doubling trajectory.

shifts.trend: FY26 interconnect base → FY27 guided >70% YoY (upgraded from 50%) → scale-up optics FY28 target 'more than double' prior $150M outlook (implying $300M+); DCI target $1B annualized in FY28
shifts.note: Celestial AI CPO already selected by a Tier 1 hyperscaler for next-gen XPU scale-up; NVIDIA photonics partnership adds a second growth vector for silicon photonics.

shifts.trend: FY26 data center switch $300M+ → FY27 guided >$600M (doubling) → FY28 $1B+ annualized target; 51.2T products ramping, 100T platform sampling H1 FY27
shifts.note: Scale-out switching is becoming a meaningful revenue line in its own right, adding margin mix as ASICs and networking converge in hyperscale deployments.

shifts.trend: 5-year forecast shared with key suppliers; ~$1B supplier prepayments planned in FY27; COO confirms supply secured for FY27 and beyond despite tight advanced packaging and HBM
shifts.note: Proactive prepayment strategy mirrors AVGO's approach and signals MRVL is competing for constrained TSMC advanced node and CoWoS capacity well ahead of revenue need.

MRVL.verdict: MRVL's cross-quarter arc is the clearest in the group — every major guide has been raised, data center mix has reached 76%, and custom silicon is proving to be a sustainable double-not-a-one-time event with FY29 $10B+ target anchored by three Tier 1 customers. The principal risk is execution risk on Celestial AI CPO scale-up (ambitious $300M+ FY28 target for an acquired company) and customer concentration — Distributor A at 37-38% of revenue means one hyperscaler's capex cycle drives the print.

MSFT.headline: Azure accelerated from 39% to 40% growth while Microsoft's AI ARR surpassed $37B (+123% YoY) and commercial RPO doubled to $627B — with Q4 capex guided above $40B despite management calling Azure 'constrained through 2026'.

shifts.trend: $29.9B Q2 FY26 → $30.9B Q3 FY26; 9M FY26 total $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year; Q4 guided >$40B; CY2026 total ~$190B including ~$25B from component pricing
shifts.note: The explicit $25B component-pricing surcharge in the CY2026 guidance is the clearest hyperscaler disclosure that memory/silicon inflation is not being absorbed — it is being passed through to capex budgets and ultimately to supply-chain participants.

shifts.trend: Azure +39% Q2 FY26 → +40% Q3 FY26; Microsoft Cloud $51.5B Q2 → $54.5B Q3 (+29% YoY)
shifts.note: The one-point Azure acceleration in Q3 despite constrained capacity — driven by earlier-than-expected compute delivery — implies pent-up demand that will release further as the 2026 capex build comes online.

shifts.trend: Microsoft AI business >$37B ARR as of Q3 FY26, up 123% YoY; M365 Copilot paid seats exceed 20M with seat adds +250% YoY
shifts.note: A $37B AI ARR growing at 123% — with Copilot seat adds tripling YoY — demonstrates that Microsoft's AI monetization is running well ahead of the infrastructure build, creating a durable demand floor for continued capex.

shifts.trend: Commercial RPO $625B at Dec 31 2025 (+110% YoY) → $627B at Mar 31 2026 (+99% YoY)
shifts.note: Over $600B of contracted future revenue — approximately 2.5 years of recognition at current run rates — provides near-certain capex justification through FY28 and beyond, locking in data center, silicon, and power commitments.

shifts.trend: Azure capacity constrained through 2026; management added 'another gigawatt of capacity this quarter' and is 'on track to double overall footprint in 2 years'
shifts.note: The explicit supply-constraint admission — capacity delivered earlier in Q3 enabled incremental Azure consumption acceleration — is the most direct evidence that GPU and data-center availability, not customer demand, is the current growth ceiling.

shifts.trend: Maia 200 AI accelerator live in Iowa and Arizona data centers; >30% improved tokens per dollar vs prior-gen fleet silicon
shifts.note: Maia deployment alongside NVIDIA systems signals Microsoft is replicating the hyperscaler pattern of custom silicon for inference cost reduction while continuing to purchase NVIDIA for training and frontier workloads — a bifurcated demand signal for the GPU and ASIC layers.

MSFT.verdict: Microsoft is supply-constrained, not demand-constrained: Azure accelerated despite limited capacity, AI ARR is growing at 123%, and RPO stands at $627B — yet management explicitly flagged that Azure will remain constrained through 2026. The 9-month capex of $80.1B against a prior-year $40.9B base and a Q4 guided above $40B puts CY2026 on track for ~$190B in infrastructure spend, of which $25B is explicitly attributed to component pricing inflation. For the inference stack, this is the most supply-tightness-corroborating signal in the cohort: a hyperscaler with >$600B of contracted obligations admitting it cannot serve all demand today means power, cooling, networking, and silicon layers have multi-year demand locked in regardless of macro conditions.

MU.headline: Two-quarter beat-and-raise cadence accelerating — Q2 revenue beat guide by 28%, EPS by 45%; Q3 guide implies further +40% QoQ.

shifts.note: QoQ growth re-accelerated from +21% to +75% and is guided to +40% again
shifts.trend: Revenue $13.6B → $23.9B actual → $33.5B guide (YoY: +57% → +196% → implied +250%+)

shifts.note: Pricing power compounding — AI demand outpaces industry supply
shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 56.8% → 74.9% → ~81% guide (≈ +24 pts of expansion in two quarters)

shifts.note: Beat magnitude widening, not narrowing — credibility on the raised Q3 guide is high
shifts.trend: Q2 revenue guided $18.7B → printed $23.9B (+28% beat); Q3 now guided to $33.5B (+40% QoQ)

shifts.note: Same constraint language for two consecutive quarters; tightness is persistent, not transient
shifts.trend: 'Supply allocation decisions may impact certain customers' (Q1) → 'Industry supply allocation decisions continue' (Q2) — no easing language added

shifts.note: HBM hyperscaler reliance increasing — single-customer dependency to monitor
shifts.trend: One customer reached 13% of six-month revenue (Q2 disclosure) — primarily CMBU (HBM/hyperscale)

shifts.note: Singapore HBM advanced-packaging fab broke ground; capacity additions from CY2027
shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$20B (Q1 guide); Q1 capex $5.39B + Q2 capex $5.0B = $10.4B run-rate H1 — on pace for $20B+

shifts.note: First meaningful dividend hike in years; mgmt signaling cycle durability
shifts.trend: Dividend $0.115/share (Q1) → $0.15/share (Q2, +30%)

MU.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — beat-and-raise cadence is widening, gross-margin expansion is compounding, and guidance credibility is high. The only emerging risk is HBM customer concentration (one customer at 13% of revenue). Supply tightness language has not eased — bullish for memory pricing through CY2026.

MXL.headline: MaxLinear pivoted from a broadband-recovery story to an optical DSP ramp narrative as Keystone PAM4 entered hyperscaler production — but with total revenue still at ~$137M, optical DC revenue at $150M-$170M full-year, and Rushmore 1.6T still in qualification, MXL is thesis-building rather than thesis-proving.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $467.6M (+30% YoY recovery) → Q4 FY25 ~$136.4M → Q1 FY26 $137.2M (+43% YoY); Q2 FY26 guide $160M-$170M (+17%-24% QoQ)
shifts.note: The +43% YoY in Q1 FY26 looks strong but the base was severely depressed — the Q2 guide of $165M midpoint implies sequential momentum that is genuinely optical-DSP-driven, not just base recovery.

shifts.trend: Infrastructure segment: +$34.3M in FY25 → +$36.3M in Q1 FY26 alone; optical DC revenue guidance initiated at $150M-$170M for full-year 2026 and raised to same range at Q1 (above initial expectations)
shifts.note: Optical DC guidance being raised within the same quarter it was initiated signals hyperscaler adoption of Keystone PAM4 is tracking above management's own models — a rare demand-side positive surprise for MXL.

shifts.trend: 2026 optical DC revenue: $130M-$150M (initial range, implied) → $150M-$170M (raised at Q4 FY25 call, maintained and confirmed at Q1 FY26); Rushmore 1.6T production ramp target late 2026
shifts.note: Rushmore 1.6T DSP entering qualification now positions MXL to participate in the next speed tier cycle, but production ramp in late 2026 means revenue contribution is a 2027 story.

shifts.trend: FY25 gross margin 57% (+3pts YoY); Q4 FY25 non-GAAP GM ~59%; Q1 FY26 non-GAAP GM 59.5%; Q2 FY26 guide 58%-61%
shifts.note: GM stabilizing in the 59%-60% range as high-ASP optical DSPs displace lower-margin broadband/IMS product mix — the mix shift thesis is working but margin upside is now incremental, not step-function.

shifts.trend: Top 10 customers: 65% of FY25 revenue → 56% of Q1 FY26 revenue; largest customer: >20% (FY25) → 13% (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: Customer base broadening as Keystone PAM4 ramps at multiple hyperscalers in US and Asia is a structural positive — less binary outcome risk than a single-customer optical DSP program.

shifts.trend: R&D spending -7% in FY25 on workforce reductions; mgmt acknowledges R&D will need to re-expand for Rushmore and next-gen products; cash $89.9M at Q1 FY26 (thin balance sheet)
shifts.note: Cash at $89.9M is the tightest balance sheet in this peer group — MXL needs Keystone ramp revenue to fund Rushmore development; any program delay creates a cash-burn squeeze without equity financing.

MXL.verdict: MXL is the highest-risk/highest-optionality name in the inference optics stack: if Keystone PAM4 sustains multi-hyperscaler ramp and Rushmore 1.6T converts from qualification to production in late 2026, the company can grow from a ~$600M annualized run-rate to $1B+ on DSP content alone. The thesis is early-stage but directionally confirmed — optical DC guidance was raised on its first iteration, and customer diversification is genuinely improving. The key risks are balance sheet thinness ($89.9M cash) limiting R&D reinvestment in next-gen products, and technology risk that merchant-silicon PAM4 DSP faces substitution from custom ASIC programs at the very hyperscalers MXL is trying to penetrate.

NOW.headline: Now Assist ACV doubled YoY to $600M+ at FY25 year-end, then crossed $750M in Q1 FY26 alone, driving a FY26 target raise to $1.5B — ServiceNow has crossed from AI feature to AI monetization at scale.

shifts.trend: FY24 Now Assist ACV baseline → FY25 year-end $600M+ (doubled YoY) → Q1 FY26 crossed $750M → FY26 target raised to $1.5B (from $1.0B)
shifts.note: ACV trajectory is well ahead of the raised target pace — if Q1 added ~$150M QoQ from $600M, reaching $1.5B by December 2026 requires only ~$250M per remaining quarter.

shifts.trend: FY25 subscription $12.9B +21% YoY → Q1 FY26 subscription $3.67B +22% YoY; FY26 target ~$15.5B (beating the $15B 2021 target organically)
shifts.note: 22% growth acceleration in Q1 FY26 coming off a 21% FY25 base is notable — Rule of 60+ metric (growth + FCF margin) should be achievable in FY26 ahead of the 2030 target.
Slight RPO moderation (Q1 $27.7B vs FY25-end $28.2B) is seasonal; YoY growth of 25% is structurally healthy and reflects multi-year AI agent platform commitments.

shifts.trend: FY25: 91% of net new ACV from deals with 5+ products (vs 86% FY24); Security & Risk net new ACV +40% YoY; AI Control Tower ACV 4x since launch; CRM ACV crossed $1.8B
shifts.note: Multi-product attach is the most durable competitive moat signal — customers embedding ServiceNow in 5+ workflows have extraordinarily high switching costs.

shifts.trend: FY25: $100M internal productivity savings from agentic AI; Q4 FY25: 90% of customer support cases handled by agents → FY26 target $300M+ annualized savings; EmployeeWorks pipeline 10x in 2.5 months post-launch
shifts.note: Internal productivity results serve as proof-of-concept for enterprise customers and a direct sales tool — measurable outcomes are exactly what CFOs demand before expanding AI contracts.

shifts.trend: Prior FY30 target → Analyst Day May 2026: $30B+ subscription (baseline) / $32B (higher scenario); Rule of 60+ by 2030; SBC below 10% of revenue by 2029
shifts.note: The $30B target implies ~16% CAGR from FY26 $15.5B — achievable at current growth rates, with Action Fabric (open API consumption layer for external AI agents) as the key incremental revenue driver.

NOW.verdict: Thesis most intact of any software name in this group: Now Assist ACV is tracking dramatically ahead of prior targets, the multi-product attach rate is hardening switching costs, and the $30B FY30 target is grounded in observable ACV trajectories rather than aspirational TAM math. Action Fabric (the open API layer for external AI agents to trigger ServiceNow workflows) is the most credible new monetization vector in the cohort — consumption revenue from agents firing ServiceNow actions could be a step-change above the current subscription ceiling. Key risk is execution in the government and regulated-industry segments, where AI agent deployment cycles are longer and the federal budget environment is unpredictable.

NVDA.headline: NVDA's Blackwell is fully ramped and Vera Rubin arrives Q3 FY27 — networking at $14.8B (+199% YoY) is now a second GPU-sized revenue engine, and the $1 trillion Blackwell+Rubin cycle is just beginning.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 DC $51.2B (+66% YoY) → FY26 DC +68% YoY → Q1 FY27 guidance $43.0B total (flat QoQ, post-record Q4) → Q2 FY27 guided ~$45B; networking sub-segment alone $14.8B (+199% YoY) in Q1 FY27
shifts.note: The networking segment is growing nearly 3x faster than compute, confirming that the NVLink/InfiniBand/Spectrum-X attach rate is rising structurally as cluster sizes scale.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26: Blackwell Ultra now leading architecture, Hopper transitioning → FY26 full year: Blackwell majority of DC revenue → Q1 FY27: full production, gross margin recovering → Q3 FY27: Vera Rubin volume production begins
shifts.note: Annual cadence is intact — Blackwell peak revenue expected across FY27 before Vera Rubin transitions, sustaining a $1T+ combined revenue cycle through 2027 per management's own framing.

shifts.trend: Prior-year Q3 China $8.1B → Q3 FY26 China $3.0B (H20 banned Apr 2025) → FY26 10-K: $4.5B H20 inventory charge in Q1 FY26 → Feb 2026 H200 license with 25% tariff
shifts.note: China is structurally impaired as a revenue driver but partial license relief provides optionality; ex-China hyperscaler and ACIE demand is large enough to fully absorb the lost China volume.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 GM 73.4% (compressed during Hopper→Blackwell full-system transition) → FY26 10-K: sequentially recovering → Q1 FY27 10-K guided 73.5% non-GAAP
shifts.note: Blackwell cost-per-token is improving each quarter as yields scale; Vera Rubin's higher performance density should sustain margins above 73% into FY27.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26: 4 direct customers each >10% revenue (22%/15%/13%/11%); Q1 FY27: hyperscalers and AI clouds (ACIEs) each ~50% of DC revenue
shifts.note: The 50/50 hyperscaler/ACIE split diversifies demand — AI clouds are the faster-growing segment and less correlated to any single hyperscaler capex cycle.

shifts.trend: $17.5B invested in private companies/infra funds + $3.5B land/power guarantees → $124B in purchase commitments locked with suppliers 3+ years forward
shifts.note: The $124B supplier commitment signals that NVDA is pre-buying CoWoS, HBM, and substrate capacity years in advance, effectively rationing supply to its most strategic customers.

NVDA.verdict: NVDA's thesis is intact at a new order of magnitude — $14.8B networking revenue in a single quarter and a guided $45B Q2 total with Vera Rubin arriving in Q3 FY27 all confirm the platform moat is widening, not narrowing. The primary risks are: (1) a China policy overhang that has permanently removed ~$5B/quarter of high-margin H20 revenue, and (2) customer concentration — four accounts represent 61% of Q3 FY26 revenue, making any hyperscaler capex pause a direct earnings event.

NVT.headline: nVent's infrastructure segment grew ~80% organically in Q1 FY26, new products contributed over 20 percentage points of that growth, and the full-year organic guide was doubled from 10–13% to 21–23% after the first quarter — an historically rare mid-cycle guidance revision of this magnitude.

shifts.trend: FY25 Systems Protection organic ~17%; Q4 FY25 infrastructure organic mid-teens; Q1 FY26 Systems Protection organic +50.1% (+76% total including EPG acquisition); infrastructure vertical ~+80% organic in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: The infrastructure organic growth rate nearly tripled from Q4 FY25 to Q1 FY26, confirming the acceleration is demand-driven, not acquisition-driven.

shifts.trend: Infrastructure crossed 50% of total revenue in Q4 FY25; >55% of quarterly revenue in Q1 FY26 — structural mix shift accelerating; infrastructure drove ~46 percentage points of Systems Protection organic growth in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Infrastructure is now the majority of nVent's revenue and growing fastest — the company's center of gravity has durably shifted toward data center enclosures and liquid cooling.

shifts.trend: Backlog ~$2.3B at YE25 with book-to-bill above 1.0x → backlog $2.6B in Q1 FY26 (up low-double-digits sequentially, book-to-bill 1.2x); orders +~40% organic in Q1, mostly AI data center
shifts.note: The sequential backlog expansion alongside a 1.2x book-to-bill demonstrates orders are outpacing the already-elevated revenue run rate — demand is building, not plateauing.

shifts.trend: Systems Protection margin 22.1% (FY24) → 20.7% (FY25, −1.4pts on capacity investment + tariffs) → 22.7% (Q1 FY26, +220bps vs Q1 FY25); consolidated gross margin 37.7% (FY25) → 35.9% (Q1 FY26, −290bps on tariff/mix dilution)
shifts.note: Segment-level margin recovery is encouraging but gross margin declined on tariffs and capacity investment — the net picture is improving at the operating level on volume leverage while the gross line remains under pressure.

shifts.trend: FY26 initial guide (Feb 2026): organic growth 10–13%, adj EPS $3.90–$4.05 → post-Q1 raise (May 2026): organic growth 21–23%, adj EPS $4.45–$4.55; Q2 guide organic +23–25%
shifts.note: Doubling the organic growth guidance after one quarter is an extraordinary signal — it implies management had visibility into demand that even their initial guide did not fully capture.

shifts.trend: New products cited as meaningful FY25 driver; new products contributed >20 percentage points of Q1 FY26 sales growth (liquid cooling and high-density enclosure solutions)
shifts.note: The >20pp new-product contribution is structurally significant — it suggests nVent's growth is not purely share-of-wallet expansion but reflects new product cycles aligned with next-generation GPU pod architecture.

NVT.verdict: The AI data center thesis for nVent is intensifying beyond initial expectations — the doubling of the full-year organic guide from 10–13% to 21–23% after Q1 represents a rare mid-cycle demand revelation, driven by both existing products (enclosures) and new products (liquid cooling) contributing over 20 percentage points of growth. The business mix shift (infrastructure now >55% of revenue) is durable and the Avail EPG acquisition has broadened the addressable market. Key risks are tariff headwinds ($80M FY26 estimate), gross margin pressure (−290bps in Q1 from inflation + capacity), and execution on the Blaine, MN facility ramp; at the revenue trajectory implied by a 21–23% organic year, these risks appear manageable relative to the demand tailwind.

OKLO.headline: Oklo crossed from LOI-accumulation to active multi-site construction and a first hyperscaler prepayment, with a $2.5B war chest funding simultaneous Aurora, fuel, and isotope build-outs through 2028.

shifts.trend: ~18,100 MWe (Sep 2025, +2,500% since Jul 2023) with 12 GW Switch master agreement and new data center LOIs → 1.2 GW Meta prepayment agreement (Jan 5, 2026, Pike County Ohio) — first hyperscaler cash commitment to advanced fission
shifts.note: The pivot from non-binding LOIs to a binding prepayment with Meta is a qualitative step-change; Meta providing upfront capital de-risks the deployment schedule and validates the commercial model.

shifts.trend: NRC Phase I pre-application: no significant gaps (Jul 2025) → PDC topical report accepted on accelerated timeline (Sep 2025) → Aurora Nuclear Safety Design Agreement approved by DOE (Feb 2026) → NRC approved Principal Design Criteria topical report (Q1 FY26) → PDSA submitted to DOE (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: Every quarter added a concrete NRC or DOE milestone; the dual-track regulatory path (NRC license + DOE authorization) is advancing in parallel without the gaps the market feared.

shifts.trend: Aurora-INL site selected for DOE Reactor Pilot (Aug 2025) → Pike County Ohio land acquired (late 2025) → Q1 FY26: active construction across all three business units (Power, Fuel, Isotopes); Groves isotope reactor reached substantial completion in 229 days
shifts.note: The company moved from planning to multi-site simultaneous construction in one quarter; Groves reactor targeting July 4, 2026 criticality is the nearest binary catalyst.

shifts.trend: 9M FY25 cash used in operations $48.7M (guidance $65–80M FY25) → FY26 operating burn guided $80–100M + CapEx $350–450M; Q1 FY26 cash/securities $2.5B (up from $1.18B Sep 2025)
shifts.note: Cash burn is rising sharply as construction ramps, but the $1.2B ATM completion in Q1 and the Meta prepayment mean liquidity is not a near-term constraint; the balance sheet is sized for the deployment plan.

shifts.trend: Atomic Alchemy acquisition (Feb 2025) → 3 DOE Reactor Pilot selections (Aug 2025) → Equinix/Diamondback/Prometheus LOIs → Meta prepayment (Jan 2026) → NVIDIA + Los Alamos AI fuel validation; Battelle/INL Project Pluto; Oklo-LEU JV exploration (Mar 2026)
shifts.note: The ecosystem is broadening beyond data centers into defense (Eielson AFB), isotopes, and fuel supply — the Oklo-Centrus JV on HALEU deconversion is particularly notable as it would lock up the only US-capable fuel supply chain adjacent to the 1.2 GW Ohio campus.

OKLO.verdict: The thesis is on track: Oklo has navigated from SPAC announcement to active construction with a pristine balance sheet and the first binding hyperscaler prepayment in advanced fission history. Key risks are regulatory schedule slippage at NRC (PDSA review timing is uncertain) and first-of-kind construction cost overruns at Aurora-INL. The 2028 first-deployment target remains unchanged across all three filings — any slip to 2029+ would reset the demand-capture narrative given competing SMR timelines.

ORCL.headline: Oracle's RPO surged from $97B to $553B in four quarters — a 5.7x increase — while OCI AI infrastructure revenue grew 243% YoY and management secured 10+ gigawatts of power capacity, marking Oracle's emergence as a Tier-1 AI infrastructure platform.

shifts.trend: $97.3B Nov 2024 → $523.3B Nov 2025 → $552.6B Feb 2026 (+468% YoY at Q3); $29B+ of new contracts signed in Q3 alone
shifts.note: The 5.7x RPO expansion in four quarters — from $97B to $553B — is the largest disclosed backlog acceleration in enterprise tech history; it reflects Oracle winning multi-year AI infrastructure contracts that are structurally different from its legacy software maintenance book.

shifts.trend: Cloud infrastructure $4.08B Q2 FY26 (+68% YoY) → $4.89B Q3 FY26 (+84% YoY, +81% constant currency)
shifts.note: Sequential acceleration from 68% to 84% YoY growth while management explicitly states 'demand continues to exceed supply' means OCI is adding new capacity and immediately filling it — the growth ceiling is construction pace, not customer demand.

shifts.trend: OCI AI infrastructure revenue +243% YoY in Q3 FY26; Multicloud Database revenue +531% YoY in Q3 FY26
shifts.note: Triple-digit AI infrastructure growth alongside a 5x Multicloud Database expansion signals Oracle has cracked both the compute-as-a-service (OCI GPU clusters) and data-gravity (multicloud database colocation) vectors of the AI infrastructure market simultaneously.

shifts.trend: H1 FY26 capex $20.5B vs $6.3B H1 FY25 (3.2x); 9M FY26 $39.2B vs $12.1B (3.2x); trend to continue 'next few fiscal years'; $47.7B raised in debt/equity (senior notes + mandatory convert)
shifts.note: Oracle is funding a capex build that is 3x its prior-year pace almost entirely with external capital — $42.7B in senior notes plus $5B mandatory convertible preferred — supported by the RPO book as collateral; this leverage structure is only viable if the contracted revenue materializes.

shifts.trend: Secured >10 GW of power and data center capacity coming online over next 3 years; >90% of that capacity fully funded through partners; Q3 delivered >400 MW to customers
shifts.note: 10 GW of secured power — with 90% partner-funded — is a differentiated model: Oracle is effectively acting as a capital-light aggregator of AI infrastructure capacity, using its RPO book to backstop third-party data center and power development.

shifts.trend: AI gross margin for capacity delivered in Q3 = 32%, above the 30% guidance floor; Multicloud and database segments carry 60-80% margins
shifts.note: Sustaining 32% gross margin on freshly delivered AI GPU capacity — while 9-month capex tripled YoY — confirms the OCI unit economics are intact even in the early high-depreciation phase; margin expansion is expected as utilization matures.

ORCL.verdict: Oracle's transformation from legacy software vendor to Tier-1 AI infrastructure platform is now clearly visible in the financials: RPO at $553B (5.7x YoY), OCI AI infrastructure at 243% growth, and a 9-month capex of $39.2B funded by $47.7B of new debt issuance. The 'bring-your-own-hardware' and 'upfront-payment' contract structures Oracle is winning represent a fundamentally different demand vector from the traditional cloud: customers are pre-purchasing GPU capacity years in advance, giving Oracle forward revenue certainty that justifies the aggressive debt-funded buildout. For the inference stack, Oracle's 10 GW power pipeline and partner-funded data center model create direct, multi-year demand for power infrastructure, cooling, networking, and silicon — and the $29B+ of new contracts signed in a single quarter suggests the Q3 momentum is compounding rather than plateauing.

PLTR.headline: Palantir's US commercial revenue surged +133% YoY in Q1 FY26, Rule of 40 hit 145%, and the company raised FY26 revenue guidance to $7.65B — AIP is converting from proof-of-concept to enterprise production at a rate that has no precedent in the company's history.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY25 US commercial >50% YoY → FY25 commercial $2.07B +60% YoY → Q1 FY26 US commercial $595M +133% YoY
shifts.note: The acceleration from >50% in Q3 FY25 to +133% in Q1 FY26 is the most dramatic growth inflection of any company in this cohort — AIP bootcamp-driven enterprise adoption is clearly past the pilot phase.

shifts.trend: FY25 $4.5B +53% YoY (blended gov + commercial) → Q1 FY26 $1.633B +85% YoY; Q2 FY26 guidance $1.797B–$1.801B (+80%+ implied); FY26 full-year raised to $7.65B
shifts.note: Growth is accelerating at scale — an 85% YoY rate on a $1.6B quarterly run-rate is exceptional, and the $7.65B FY26 guide implies sustained ~80% growth through year-end.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY25 Rule of 40 = 127% → Q1 FY26 Rule of 40 = 145% (highest ever); Q1 FY26 adjusted operating margin 60%; FCF $925M (57% margin)
shifts.note: Operating margin expansion from ~20% (Q1 FY25) to 46% GAAP / 60% adjusted in Q1 FY26 while revenue doubled indicates Palantir has achieved significant operating leverage on its software platform.

shifts.trend: Q1 FY25 gross margin ~80% → Q1 FY26 87% (GAAP), 88% adjusted ex-SBC
shifts.note: Gross margin expansion to 87–88% as revenue scales confirms that AIP/Foundry/Gotham are pure-software businesses with minimal incremental delivery cost.

shifts.trend: FY25 government $2.40B +53% YoY → Q1 FY26 government $858M +76% YoY; contribution margin expanded from 60% (FY25) to 73% (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: Government growth accelerating despite already being the larger segment, driven by defense AI deployments; contribution margin expansion (+13pp YoY) is striking and suggests platform pricing power.

shifts.trend: FY25 US revenue 74% of total → Q1 FY26 US $1.282B = 79% of total (+104% YoY); international $350M (21%)
shifts.note: Increasing US concentration is a double-edged signal: US market capture is exceptionally strong, but international commercial penetration remains limited — geographic diversification risk is rising.

PLTR.verdict: Thesis intact at an extraordinary inflection point — Q1 FY26 US commercial +133% YoY and Rule of 40 of 145% are not incremental data points, they are regime-change signals that AIP has crossed the enterprise adoption chasm. FCF at $925M in a single quarter (exceeding total company revenue from Q1 FY25) demonstrates the business model's operating leverage is now structurally intact. Key risk is valuation: at $7.65B FY26 revenue guidance and current market cap, the multiple is pricing in sustained high-80%+ growth beyond FY26, which requires international commercial to begin scaling (currently 21% of revenue) and AIP bootcamp economics to hold as deployments move from pilot to enterprise-wide. A second risk is the US government dependency (~54% of FY25 revenue) — any DoD/intelligence budget compression or contract re-competition could materially disrupt the growth trajectory despite commercial strength.

PWR.headline: Technology and load-center work accelerated from 70% growth in FY25 to 100%+ growth in Q1 FY26, backlog hit a record $48.5B with a 1.6x book-to-bill, and Quanta raised FY26 guidance while reconfirming a target to double earnings power by 2030 — the AI-driven infrastructure services cycle is in full force.

shifts.trend: FY24: data center campus work described as growing; FY25: technology and load-center work grew ~70% YoY; Q1 FY26: technology and load-center work growing 100%+ YoY (accelerated from 70%); DSI and CEI acquisitions cited as primary growth vehicles
shifts.note: The sequential acceleration from 70% in FY25 to 100%+ in Q1 FY26 — on an already-elevated base — is the most significant demand-velocity signal in Quanta's filing history.
Backlog and RPO are growing both YoY and sequentially, meaning new awards are meaningfully outpacing revenue recognition — the book is building, not being consumed.

shifts.trend: Book-to-bill was already strong entering FY26; Q1 FY26 book-to-bill 1.6x — broad-based across electric power, technology and oil & gas; first meaningful 765kV MSA signed in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: A 1.6x book-to-bill at $7.9B quarterly revenue implies orders of approximately $12–13B in Q1 alone — with data center and 765kV transmission as the leading contributors.

shifts.trend: FY25 Electric segment +21% to $23.0B; Q1 FY26 total revenues +26.3% to $7.87B; Electric segment +$1.52B in Q1; Q2 FY26 expected to maintain strong pace
shifts.note: Revenue growth is reaccelerating into an already-strong FY25 base, driven by the convergence of AI campus electrical work and grid infrastructure — two secular demand streams operating simultaneously.

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 gross margin 14.1% (vs 13.4%); operating margin 4.3% (vs 3.8%); net income +55% to $231M; adj EPS $2.68 (+30%); Underground margin expanded from 5.7% to 7.3% in FY25
shifts.note: Margin trajectory is improving as higher-value technology and data center work scales within the mix — Quanta's adj EPS growth (+30%) is running well ahead of revenue growth (+26%), confirming operating leverage.

shifts.trend: FY26 initial guide (Feb 2026): revenue $34.5–35.0B, adj EBITDA $3.4–3.6B, adj EPS $13.00–13.75 → post-Q1 raise: revenue $34.7–35.2B, adj EBITDA $3.49–3.65B, adj EPS $13.55–14.25; 2030 Investor Day target: double earnings power, 15–20% adj EPS CAGR confirmed
shifts.note: The full-year raise across all three key metrics (revenue, EBITDA, EPS) following a quarter with 1.6x book-to-bill suggests the demand environment is not showing signs of pull-forward — it is sustaining above initial expectations.

PWR.verdict: Quanta Services is the essential infrastructure contractor for the AI power build-out — it builds the campus electrical systems, grid connections (including 765kV), and modular inside-the-fence infrastructure that turn hyperscaler capex commitments into operating data centers. The combination of a record $48.5B backlog (1.6x book-to-bill), technology/load-center work accelerating to 100%+ growth, and a $500–700M transformer manufacturing investment to solve a key bottleneck positions Quanta to compound above the market for multiple years. Key risks are labor and skilled-craft workforce availability (chronic in this sector) and the capital intensity of the $500–700M transformer investment; the 15–20% adj EPS CAGR target to 2030 is credible given current backlog duration but depends on execution at scale.

SNOW.headline: Cortex Code launched Feb 5 and drove Snowflake's strongest sequential dollar growth in company history — product revenue accelerated to 34% YoY in Q1 FY27, NRR inflected back up to 126%, and the AI product flywheel is pulling core platform consumption.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 $1.16B +29% YoY → FY26 $4.5B (implied +31%) → Q1 FY27 $1.334B +34% YoY (highest acceleration in years); Q2 FY27 guidance $1.415–$1.420B (+30%)
shifts.note: The Cortex Code (CoCo) launch on Feb 5 contributed disproportionately to the Q1 FY27 upside — 7,100+ accounts adopted in the first quarter, driving both direct AI revenue and second-order core platform consumption.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 NRR 125% (vs 126% at Jan FY25) → FY26 year-end 125% → Q1 FY27 126% (first sequential improvement in multiple quarters)
shifts.note: NRR inflecting upward after multi-quarter moderation is a critical signal that AI workloads are expanding existing customer consumption rather than just adding new logos.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26: AI/ML workload category nascent → Q1 FY27: 79 customers spending >$1M on AI products (TTM); 46 crossed $1M threshold in Q1 vs 26 year-ago; 7,100+ CoCo accounts in first 90 days
shifts.note: The 79-customer cohort at >$1M AI spend is the key metric to track — if it continues doubling annually, AI products become a multi-hundred-million revenue layer within 2–3 quarters.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 RPO $7.9B (+24% implied) → FY26 year-end $9.8B (+24% YoY) → Q1 FY27 RPO growing +38% YoY (vs +34% in Q1 FY26); $6B multiyear AWS deal signed
shifts.note: RPO growth accelerating above product revenue growth (+38% vs +34%) signals that contracted future consumption is building faster than current recognition — a setup for sustained growth.

shifts.trend: Q3 FY26 non-GAAP operating margin mid-single digits → Q1 FY27 12% (+300bp YoY); FY27 guidance raised to 13.5% (from 12.5%); only 17 organic hires in Q1 FY27
shifts.note: Extreme hiring discipline (17 organic additions in Q1 FY27) is converting revenue acceleration into operating leverage at a pace the market has not seen from Snowflake historically.

shifts.trend: FY25: 576 customers with >$1M trailing revenue → FY26: 733 (+27% YoY) → Q1 FY27: 64 customers >$10M TTM (8 new in Q1); 13 Global 2000 additions vs 4 year-ago
shifts.note: Enterprise deepening (>$10M cohort expanding, Global 2000 logos accelerating) validates that Snowflake is winning the AI data platform seat at the largest accounts.

SNOW.verdict: Thesis strongly reinforcing: Cortex Code has done what AI features rarely do — it generated measurable revenue acceleration in the first quarter of availability and pulled forward core platform consumption simultaneously. The NRR inflection to 126% after multiple quarters of moderation is the most important data point — it suggests the negative mix effect from new small-customer cohorts is being offset by AI-driven expansion in the existing base. Key risk is gross margin — AI products carry lower margin than core platform (75% FY27 guidance vs historically higher core), meaning revenue acceleration may not fully translate to operating income expansion if AI mix grows faster than anticipated. The $6B AWS deal and $200M OpenAI partnership are strategic bets that further concentrate Snowflake's ecosystem dependencies.

SNPS.headline: Ansys mega-merger delivered first full-quarter contribution, Design IP troughed, and FY26 guidance raised on 41% operating margin — but $13.5B debt load and China headwinds limit near-term optionality.

shifts.trend: FY25 $7.05B (+15% organic-adjusted) → Q1 FY26 $2.41B (+66% YoY) → Q2 FY26 $2.28B; FY26 full-year guide $9.625B–$9.705B
shifts.note: Ansys contributed $885.6M in Q1 and ~$652M in Q2, inflating reported growth, but organic EDA and verification demand is independently strong.

shifts.trend: FY25 compressed by $457.8M intangible amortization → Q2 FY26 non-GAAP 39.5% → FY26 guide raised to 41% midpoint (+50bp raise)
shifts.note: ~50% of post-Ansys cost synergies expected to land by FY26 year-end, with Multiphysics Fusion revenue synergies ($400M target) deferred to FY27+.

shifts.trend: FY25 segment weak (BIS letter May 29 rescinded Jul 2) → Q1 FY26 IP -6% YoY (trough declared) → Q2 FY26 IP $454M, -6% YoY but +12% QoQ
shifts.note: Sequential recovery confirmed with new IP business models (subscription + royalty) being agreed with first hyperscaler customers by end of FY26.

shifts.trend: FY25 China revenue -22% ex-Ansys; cumulative BIS export-control whiplash (May 29 letter, Jul 2 rescission); company guiding 'muted' China and being 'pragmatic' in forecasts
shifts.note: China design starts are structurally suppressed regardless of BIS rescission — hyperscaler AI design activity and Taiwan/Korea wins are the growth offset.

shifts.trend: Backlog $8.1B (FY24) → $11.4B (FY25, including Ansys) → $11.3B (Q1 FY26) → $11.0B (Q2 FY26); 45–47% due next 12 months
shifts.note: Backlog modestly normalizing post-integration but remains ~60% higher than pre-Ansys levels, underpinning multi-year revenue visibility.

shifts.trend: AgentEngineer pilots: 20 customers evaluating >25 specialized AI agents as of Q2 FY26; monetization model transitioning to subscription + consumption; commercial model undetermined
shifts.note: Agentic EDA and Multiphysics Fusion represent the next-leg revenue synergy vectors, but neither is in revenue guidance before FY27.

SNPS.verdict: Thesis intact: Synopsys has successfully absorbed Ansys and is generating the first evidence of EDA-times-simulation cross-sell at hyperscaler and leading-edge semiconductor customers. The Design IP trough in Q1 FY26 appears genuine and the sequential recovery in Q2 confirms it. Key risk is dual: (1) $13.5B debt at FY25-end constrains capital returns and amplifies any revenue shortfall, and (2) China is structurally challenged and may not recover even without further BIS action, meaning any China upside is off the table for FY26 planning. The $400M Multiphysics Fusion revenue synergy target and AgentEngineer consumption uplift are real optionality but FY27+ — the stock is trading on execution of the Ansys integration and margin ramp, not yet on synergy optionality.

VRT.headline: Vertiv's Americas organic growth hit +44%, gross margin expanded 400bps to 37.7%, adj EPS surged 83% YoY, and the company raised FY26 guidance to $13.75B revenue and $6.35 EPS — every metric is inflecting simultaneously.

shifts.trend: FY25 net sales +27.7% to $10.23B; Q1 FY26 net sales +30.1% to $2.65B; Americas Q1 FY26 +53.1% YoY
shifts.note: Growth is re-accelerating into an already-elevated base, with the Americas region — the hyperscaler-facing leg — running at roughly double the reported consolidated rate.

shifts.trend: Combined order backlog $7.2B at YE24 → $15.0B at YE25 (more than doubled) → backlog entering Q2 FY26 at elevated levels supporting multi-quarter visibility; firm orders expected to ship in next 12–18 months
shifts.note: The doubling of backlog in a single year is a leading indicator that manufacturing capacity, not order flow, is the binding constraint — which supports pricing power and multi-year revenue visibility.

shifts.trend: Americas operating margin 24.4% (FY24) → 26.8% (FY25) → 27.0% (Q1 FY26); gross margin 36.6% (FY24 implied) → 36.3% (FY25, −30bps on tariffs) → 37.7% (Q1 FY26, +400bps YoY)
shifts.note: The gross margin recovery to 37.7% in Q1 FY26, reversing the FY25 tariff-driven compression, is the most significant margin signal — operating leverage is clearly visible at this revenue scale.

shifts.trend: Q1 FY25 adj EPS ~$0.64 (implied) → Q1 FY26 adj EPS $1.17 (+83%); adj FCF $653M (+147%); FY26 guide adj EPS $6.35 (+51% YoY)
shifts.note: The magnitude of EPS and FCF growth suggests the business has crossed an operating leverage inflection point, not merely benefiting from volume.

shifts.trend: Initial FY26 guide (Feb 2026): net sales ~$13.5B, adj EPS ~$6.00, adj op margin ~23% → post-Q1 raise: net sales $13.75B, adj EPS $6.35 (+5.8%), adj op margin 23.3%; Q2 FY26 guide: $3.35B revenue, $1.40 EPS
shifts.note: A meaningful guide raise this early in the year, combined with a Q2 step-up in both revenue and EPS, indicates Q1 was not a pull-forward but a new run rate.

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $226.4M → FY26 guided $425–525M (+88–132%); Q1 FY26 capex $114M already tracking to the high end of annual guidance
shifts.note: The near-doubling of capex signals Vertiv is racing to build capacity ahead of demand; the investment creates barriers to entry and supports multi-year revenue compounding.

VRT.verdict: Vertiv is the most direct pure-play on AI data center power and cooling infrastructure, and every leading indicator — backlog, orders, margins, FCF — is inflecting upward simultaneously. The FY26 guide of $13.75B revenue at 23.3% adj operating margin with $6.35 EPS (+51%) appears achievable given Q1 performance and the Q2 step-up guide. Key risks are EMEA softness (−29% organic in Q1 FY26, though order recovery is cited), execution risk on doubling capex, and potential tariff exposure; the balance sheet (net leverage 0.2x, $2.15B cash) provides ample cushion. The 800V DC power center (end-2026 ready) and Cool Loop trim cooler represent the next technology cycle, keeping Vertiv relevant as GPU pod densities increase.
